data.title: HS2 ‘will mean more traffic on clogged-up roads’
data.description:
HS2 threatens to add to the number of trucks on the road by worsening congestion on existing tracks, the boss of Britain’s biggest rail freight firm has warned.

Tim Shoveller, the chief executive of Freightliner Group, said that because it ends at Birmingham, HS2 is unlikely to ease pressure on the West Coast Main Line, a key route for cargo.

At the same time, the connection between HS2 and the West Coast Main Line at Handsacre Junction, Staffordshire, will force trains to slow, creating a bottleneck that will reduce capacity below current levels, Mr Shoveller added.

He said: “With HS2 running only from London to Birmingham it’s very difficult to see that any traffic will move from domestic rail.

“And, frankly, the connection at Handsacre will actually mean there’s a net capacity reduction on the railways because it’s a really suboptimal design.

“It’s a bit like building a new road alongside a motorway but still getting to the same roundabout at the end, with all the congestion that involves.”

Mr Shoveller said pressure was “rightly” mounting on the Government to review how to make the best of HS2 and avoid pushing shipments onto crowded roads.

HS2 was originally intended to shorten journeys between London and the North while freeing up capacity on the West Coast Main Line for slower passenger trains and freight.

However, following Rishi Sunak’s decision to scrap the northern reaches of the line in 2023, services to Manchester, Liverpool and Glasgow may have to stick to the existing route.

At the same time, trains that join the West Coast Main Line from HS2 at Handsacre Junction will have to use slow lines usually devoted to freight.

Modelling by economists at MDS Transmodal suggests that, at best, freight capacity to the North West and Scotland will be capped at current levels.

That could act as a barrier to the Government’s target of growing rail freight by 75pc by 2050 as part of its push for net zero.

Freight trains cut carbon emissions by between 76pc and 95pc per ton compared with road haulage, Mr Shoveller said.

Mr Shoveller also appealed to the Government to end a freeze in HGV fuel duty, which he said combined with higher track access charges had led to rail costs rising three times faster than for road over the past decade.

That has seen the distance over which moving containers by rail from southern ports is more competitive extended by 80 miles from Birmingham to Manchester, he said.

He said it is also vital that the Office of Rail and Road remain an independent arbiter as the Government nationalises passenger services and combines them with Network Rail – which sets charges and allocates train paths – to create Great British Railways.

The Government has been approached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: In 60 minutes I saw 172 cyclists run these lights – it’s a matter of time before they kill someone
data.description:
Spend a single hour during the morning rush standing at a set of traffic lights circling Bank Junction in the City of London, and it becomes clear that, for many cyclists, red means go.

It means go for two women who whizz through on push bikes, with empty child seats behind them rather concerningly indicating a completed school run. It means go for the commuter singing loudly from his rental Lime e-bike; the chap in high-vis gear clutching two carrier bags precariously on his handlebars; the young schoolgirl in skirt and socks; the push biker who weaves around a pedestrian wearing a “Baby on Board” badge.

Between 7.30am and 8.30am on a Thursday, The Telegraph counted these cyclists among 172 who jumped a red light on King William Street. This equals a rate of nearly three a minute.

If each was fined the £50 fixed penalty notice (FPN), which can be issued for the offence of failing to stop for a red light on a bicycle (and technically, an amber, too), then £8,600 would be raised. That figure would be even higher if those riding on pavements, two-to-a-bike without holding the handlebars – all of which we observe, and are offences, too – were fined as well.

If you were to double that figure to account for the two hours of the morning commuter rush, and double it again for the same evening commuter period, you would muster £34,400 each weekday for those times alone. That is £8,703,200 during a regular work year. Not to mention, there are three additional sets of lights positioned around Bank Junction.

According to a new survey commissioned by e-bike provider Lime, more than half of London’s cyclists run red lights. In 2024, 1.3 million bike journeys were recorded per day in the capital, which makes for a lot of red-light jumping. Furthermore, 82 per cent admitted they recognised it is dangerous. The survey included general e-bike and regular bike users, and not just Lime customers.

Last month, it was reported that the City of London Police are so concerned by the proliferation of offenders, they are exploring the introduction of tougher penalties. These include community protection warnings and notices, which treat the behaviour as anti-social, and could result in court appearances and criminal convictions for repeat offenders.

The measures were discussed at a meeting of City of London Corporation’s Streets and Walkways Sub-Committee, following Tube strikes earlier this month. Those strikes saw further mayhem as usually infrequent cyclists swarmed the roads. The London Ambulance Service reported a 33 per cent rise in collisions involving cyclists during that time.

At Bank Junction, so many cyclists jump each red light – some at high speed without any hesitation – that you start to believe the practice is as much a commuting ritual as AirPods and clutched coffee cups. The dangers of red-light jumping, however, put it in a league of its own.

“A cyclist ran over my foot when I was crossing here, he rode straight through and yelled at me to ‘get out the way,’” says Sarah Beale, 39, a personal assistant who commutes from Chelmsford. “You have to cross with your wits about you,” she adds, looking like her head might swivel off as she glances both ways on repeat. “It’s scary. I was crossing at a green man, what did I do wrong? It’s often the people on rented bikes going through the lights.”

Are they ignorant of the rules of the road? “I think it’s that they don’t care.” Beale got away with bruising. “It’s not just a London thing, but it’s busier here. We get a fine as a motorist if we go through a red light. It might stop them if the fine was more.”

Just minutes later, I meet a second casualty. John Rowland, 42, a political consultant from Weybridge, Surrey, also has a story to tell as he waits to cross.

“I was hit by a cyclist who ran a red light when I was crossing at a green man near Buckingham Palace,” he says, removing his headphones. “It hurt. I mean, I was lucky. I was bruised and bashed on my leg. He was going at quite a pace. He came flying off his bike down the road. He got up and was really p----- off. It was a shock to him as well.” Fines should be enforced, he says vehemently.

The second The Telegraph stands by the lights and watches them turn red, readying to count, a cyclist shoots through. It is a Lime e-bike. Two more cyclists follow within the first minute. During that first 15 minutes, we see 13 whizz through.

The majority are clearly commuters in gym wear or office clothes. Around half wear helmets, and some are on collapsible bicycles designed for train journeys. Among them are a handful of what look to be illegal e-bikes – non-rentals that appear to travel faster than the legally permitted 15.5mph (above this, the power should cut out and riders must rely on pedalling). However, the overwhelming majority would not strike you as lawbreakers.

At times, up to nine cyclists stream through as amber turns to red. One young woman barely looks about her as she wobbles off nonchalantly through the red. Another, in high-heeled boots, stops and starts haphazardly.

Others go through, and then are forced to pause in the centre of the junction as double-decker buses turn. I witness some near head-on collisions between cyclists. On one occasion, a man cycles through a red, goes round the corner and almost crashes into a reversing police car.

On a number of occasions, even a green crossing man does not stop them winding around pedestrians. Strangely, everyone seems so accustomed to it I don’t witness one altercation. Pedestrians shrug. “Nightmare,” one mutters in my direction. Another, a cyclist himself, assures us he does it too but “elsewhere”, not here. That “would be dangerous”, he adds as he crosses the road, texting on his phone. His road safety proficiency on foot is almost as concerning as his cycling logic.

The City of London Police’s cycling response team regularly targets the issue on patrol. In July, it announced nearly 300 cyclists had received FPNs for going through red lights so far in 2025. Speaking to The Telegraph, Sgt Stu Ford, who leads the team, says that figure increased substantially during the last week of September when they issued 104 FPNs as part of a targeted effort, the majority for red-light jumping. “That doesn’t surprise me,” he says when we reveal how many jumpers we have counted.

Officers use their discretion when issuing fines. Capacity is not there to stop every single red-light jumper, but it is clear the police’s patience is wearing thin. “I find it hard to believe in this day and age that people don’t realise they have to stop at a red light,” says Ford. “Their answer a lot of the time is, ‘Oh, well, yeah, sorry’, or, ‘Oh, well, I thought it would be OK’. Cyclists need to understand the red light is for them as well.”

He is not convinced a £50 fine is enough of a deterrent. “It’s been £50 for quite some time. You have to ask if that’s [high] enough. Maybe not.”

Ford would like to issue prolific repeat offenders with community protection notices which, if breached, would become a criminal matter. “We want people to take it seriously. It’s not about the money it’s making… What we want is for people to stop at those red lights,” he says.

At Bank Junction many do, but a shocking number do not. The pedestrians who scatter out of the way of a cyclist who jumps a red light with, inexplicably, a bicycle tyre strapped to his unwieldy rucksack, are thankfully braced to check both ways, and check again. And then once more.

data.title: Jim Ratcliffe’s chemical plant blames net zero for dozens of job cuts
data.description:
Sir Jim Ratcliffe’s Ineos has cut a fifth of jobs at its Hull chemical plant, blaming net zero and competition from coal-fuelled Chinese imports.

Some 60 roles are being axed at the Ineos Acetyls factory in Hull, which is Europe’s largest producer of acetic acid, acetic anhydride and ethyl acetate. These are vital raw materials for a multitude of products including food preservatives, medicines, paints and detergents.

Ineos warned that rising energy costs linked to green levies and competition from Chinese companies that face no such taxes were making the plant uneconomic.

Two other plants in Germany are being closed for similar reasons. Stephen Dossett, the Ineos executive overseeing these operations, said Europe was “committing industrial suicide”.

Ineos warned: “This is a textbook case of the UK and Europe sleepwalking into deindustrialisation. Once these plants shut, they never come back.”

The company said the job losses were “a direct result of sky-high energy costs and anti-competitive trade practices”.

It added: “Dirt-cheap carbon-heavy imports from China, produced using coal and emitting up to eight times more CO2 than Ineos’s UK operations, are now flooding the market.

“These Chinese products have been blocked from entering the US by effective tariffs but face no trade barriers in the UK or Europe.”

Sir Jim, British-born but living in Monaco, is the founder and chief executive of Ineos, thought to be Britain’s biggest private company. 

The industrialist made a multi-billion pound fortune buying up unloved factories, allowing him to purchase a significant stake in his beloved Manchester United.

However, Ineos’ UK operations have recently been hit hard by the surging cost of energy, especially for gas, as chemical production requires large amounts of heat.

It recently shut down its oil refinery in Grangemouth, Scotland, and has warned that the adjacent chemicals plant – which has been loss-making for several years – is also under threat.

Ineos recently invested £30m at the Hull site to switch from natural gas to hydrogen, cutting emissions by 75pc – the equivalent of taking 160,000 cars off the road.

However, the upgrade coincided with a surge in imports from China, which has stuck with the traditional cheap but dirty approach of burning coal to make both the carbon monoxide and the heat needed for producing chemicals.

David Brooks, chief executive of Ineos Acetyls, said: “Making the decision to cut 60 roles was not taken lightly. We have explored every possible alternative but in the face of sustained pressure from energy costs, combined with unfairly low-cost imports into the UK and Europe, we’ve been left with no other choice.”

Ineos also confirmed plans to shut two production units in Rheinberg, Germany, with the loss of 175 jobs, citing “crippling energy and carbon costs and a lack of tariff protection”.

Ineos Inovyn, the division running the two German plants, said both produced chemicals that were essential to European industry.

Its allylics unit makes the key ingredient for epoxy resins vital in defence, aerospace, cars and renewable energy infrastructure – while the nearby electro chemical facility produces chlorine crucial for clean water, medicines, industrial processes and sanitation.

Mr Dossett, chief executive of Ineos Inovyn, said: “Europe is committing industrial suicide. While competitors in the US and China benefit from cheap energy, European producers are being priced out by our own policies and absence of tariff protection.

“Meanwhile, high-emission imports flood our market unchecked. It’s completely unsustainable.”

The chief executive added: “We’ve reached the point where well invested, efficient European plants are closing while global emissions rise. It’s not just economic madness. It’s environmental hypocrisy.”

Steve Elliott, the chief executive of The Chemical Industries Association, said: “We are paying billions in levies and taxes imposed on energy bills meaning strategic industries, like ours, are fast disappearing.

“Between 2021 and 2024, carbon emission within the chemical industry has reduced by 38pc and with it, production has fallen by almost exactly the same amount.

“It’s decarbonisation through de-industrialisation and, to rub more salt into the wound, the UK’s imported emissions have doubled with emissions associated with imports from China at their highest in over two decades.”

data.title: Thames Water faces rocketing demand for supplies
data.description:
Thames Water has warned that plans to build 100 new data centres across London and the South East will pile more pressure on its creaking infrastructure.

The utility giant said it had identified 108 “hyper or large” data centres that will drive up demand in its region, with bosses suggesting it will have to manage water supplies carefully to ensure there is no impact on households.

Each data centre is equivalent to thousands of homes being added to a water network, meaning the pipeline of new data centres is on par with a new small city being built.

In its annual report, Thames Water said that building data centres “needs to be carefully managed from a demand and UK growth perspective”.

The company, which is battling to avoid nationalisation amid pressure from a £17bn debt pile, has previously raised the prospect of rationing water use for data centres or charging more at peak times.

Data centres contain giant racks of computer servers that need to be cooled to avoid overheating, often with water piped in. The facilities are crucial to the rise of artificial intelligence and are a key priority for Sir Keir Starmer’s growth push.

However, the vast number being built has sparked concern among water companies such as Thames Water, which is now engaging with the Government to prevent potential shortages in future.

“The south-east of England is a water-stressed region and data centres can use a vast amount of water, equivalent to the usage of thousands of homes at peak draw,” a Thames Water spokesman said.

“With a large proportion of the proposed data centres earmarked to be built in the Thames Water region, it brings a challenge between safeguarding our finite resources while supporting the UK’s growth strategy.

“It is important that we work collaboratively to meet this challenge and to avoid exacerbating water stress and impacting service for customers and the environment.”

“We are engaging with the Government regarding the challenge of water demand related to cooling data centres and how this can be mitigated. We are also working with a number of data centre providers about opportunities to reduce demand through innovation.”

A corridor of land between London and Slough, much of which is served by Thames Water, contains Britain’s densest collection of data centres.

An independent review of the water sector last week cited data centres as one of the factors that are likely to mean water bills rising by 30pc over the next five years.

The report, by Sir Jon Cunliffe, a former deputy governor of the Bank of England, said that national infrastructure bodies should be consulted when deciding where to build them.

The Government has welcomed investment in new data centres, including designating them as critical national infrastructure.

Thames Water last week started a hosepipe ban for more than 1 million people in Gloucestershire, Oxfordshire, Berkshire and Wiltshire.

The company is also seeking to agree a rescue deal with creditors, but has warned it may fall into special administration if talks between the lenders and regulator Ofwat fail.

data.title: Thames Water crisis threatens London super-sewer
data.description:
Thames Water’s debt crisis threatens to trigger millions of pounds in losses for London’s £4.5bn super-sewer.

Tideway Tunnel, which owns the project, has sounded the alarm ahead of a looming handover later this year – with bosses fearing Thames could withhold cash in its scramble to avoid nationalisation.

The sewer owner said it is closely monitoring Thames’s plight and has sought assurances from the Government to ensure the project will not be threatened if the company collapses into special administration.

In its latest annual report, Tideway said: “We continue to note Thames Water’s evolving financial position and this has led to concern that their situation might change.

“There is a possible risk that Thames Water, given its current financial position, or a special administrator may choose not to pay an element of Tideway’s revenue.

“This would not be consistent with Thames Water’s licence and would be expected to lead to a breach and enforcement action.”

As part of its contingency plans, Tideway also considered the possibility of Thames failing to support the sewer’s operations, warning that this “could affect our ability to deliver our investment programme on time and on budget.

“If Thames Water does not comply with the revenue agreement, it could have a financial impact,” the company added.

Concerns over Thames Water’s future come as the company struggles under the weight of a £17bn debt pile.

The company admitted last month that it could enter a taxpayer-backed special administration regime if a rescue deal led by more than 100 creditors fails to succeed.

Thames’s financial crisis is in stark contrast to the completion of Tideway, which has been hailed as a rare infrastructure success story.

The 25km tunnel under the River Thames, which became fully operational earlier this year, represents the biggest improvement to London’s sewage network in 150 years.

Buried 67m deep, the concrete pipe is designed to ease pollution pressures by redirecting sewage away from the river and into a treatment works in east London.

While Thames will not own Tideway, it is expected to be charged an annual fee for its use of the sewer. This will be paid to Bazalgette, a company bankrolled by a group of banks and pension funds.

A Tideway spokesman said: “Tideway is a completely separate, independent company set up to deliver the Thames Tideway Tunnel project.

“This vital new infrastructure is now operational, and has already diverted away nearly eight million tonnes of sewage that would have historically spilled into the Thames.

“We remain confident that Tideway’s revenues are well protected given water industry legislation and the licence obligations that apply to both Thames Water and Tideway. We continue to work effectively with Thames Water to deliver the benefits of the project.”

A Thames spokesman said: “Thames Water collects revenue from customers on Tideway’s behalf and passes it on to Tideway on receipt. Tideway’s revenues are well protected given water industry legislation and the license obligations that apply to both Thames Water and Tideway. We continue to work effectively with Tideway to deliver the benefits of the project.”

data.title: Steve Reed lines up administrators for Thames Water
data.description:
Ministers have appointed City insolvency advisers to prepare for the potential collapse of Thames Water.

Steve Reed, the Environment Secretary, has selected FTI Consulting to help draw up contingency plans in the event that the struggling utility giant is placed into a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

It comes as Thames Water, which has 16 million customers across London and the South East, teeters on the brink of collapse as it struggles under a £17bn debt pile.

Its future was cast into fresh doubt in June after private equity firm KKR pulled out of a £4bn rescue deal.

The company’s creditors have put forward an alternative £17bn rescue plan, but the bailout is contingent on regulators agreeing to waive historical fines.

Mr Reed has previously suggested this would not happen, making a government intervention appear increasingly likely. It is estimated that a state bailout would cost the taxpayer as much as £4bn.

The Environment Secretary in June said Labour was stepping up preparations to temporarily nationalise Thames Water. A government source said the move was a natural consequence of this.

The move to appoint FTI, which was first reported by Sky News, places the consulting firm in prime position to act as the company’s administrator should it collapse into insolvency, though the appointment of a special administrator is ultimately a decision for the court.

A Government spokesman said: “The Government will always act in the national interest on these issues.

“The company remains financially stable, but we have stepped up our preparations and stand ready for all eventualities, including applying for a special administration regime if that were to become necessary.”

Thames is currently locked in discussions with its creditors and regulator Ofwat over a new rescue deal for the company.

Creditors have been pushing for ministers to intervene to wipe out historical fines, saying a “regulatory reset” was required to ensure the utility is financially sustainable.

Thames was handed a record £123m fine in May and creditors say it could be in line for penalties of more than £1bn relating to environmental and pollution failings.

The £17bn rescue plan involves injecting £5bn in new equity and debt and writing off billions of pounds in existing debt.

A Thames Water spokesman said: “Our focus remains on a holistic and fundamental recapitalisation, delivering a market-led solution which includes targeting investment grade credit ratings and returning the company to a stable financial foundation.

“Constructive discussions with our many stakeholders continue.”

data.title: America’s looming electricity crisis is Trump’s Achilles’ heel
data.description:
The US is in danger of losing the global AI race for the most banal and careless of reasons: it is critically short of electricity.

The country is sitting on a neglected pre-modern grid that cannot meet surging power demand for data centres, cryptocurrencies, the reshoring of semiconductor plants and the proclaimed revival of the American industrial base.

Nor can it meet the needs for air conditioning through hotter and more humid summers. While we all talk about AI, the chief cause of rising electricity use last year was for cooling. Bitcoin mining took another 2pc of US power, for no productive purpose.

The electrotech revolution is already shaping the contours of global economic and strategic dominance for the next half-century. Right now, the US is losing. Trump’s tariff war and his energy culture war are together an unmitigated disaster.

The US Energy Department said in its Resource Adequacy Report in July that the planned increase in firm electricity supply is a fifth of what is needed by 2030.

“Absent intervention, it is impossible for the nation’s bulk power system to meet AI growth while maintaining a reliable power grid,” it said.

“A failure to power the data centres needed to win the AI arms race could result in adversary nations shaping digital norms and controlling digital infrastructure. The situation necessitates a radical change.”

The Sino-American fight over these “digital norms” will be won or lost within the next five to eight years.

The Texas grid operator Ercot expects peak power demand in its region to soar from 87 gigawatts (GW) this year to 138 GW by 2030. This is physically impossible.

Texas passed a law in June giving the state emergency authority to cut off power to data centres. Ercot is already having to deploy “mobile generation plants” at exorbitant cost to avert blackouts even today.

The National Infrastructure Advisory Council (Niac) says the average age of America’s 60 million large power transformers is 38 years. Many are beyond their design life.

The US supply chain is not capable of delivering the 12 million new transformers needed each year to keep the show on the road. The backlog has risen to four years. The cost of transformers has jumped 80pc since the pandemic.

There is an acute shortage of electrical steel needed to make them. This comes mostly from China, Japan, Korea or Germany. In his infinite wisdom, Trump has imposed 50pc tariffs on steel, aluminium and copper. That cost shock has yet to feed through.

Hitachi will be building a new transformer plant in Virginia, but this will not move the needle before the end of the decade.

The Niac says it is a similar tale for “other critical grid components such as conduit, smart meters, switchgear and high-voltage circuit breakers”.

The US power industry has lost its ecosystem of suppliers and forgotten how to expand after 20 years of flat demand. It needs to find another half a million skilled workers within four years.

The quickest way to generate more power is to roll out wind and solar. Trump is actively intervening to obstruct this. Maga wants coal instead.

This week the administration announced $625m of taxpayer subsidy to “expand and reinvigorate” the coal industry. It will open new federal lands, slash the royalty fees for extracting coal, and lift curbs on mercury pollution.

“In addition to drill, baby drill, we need to mine, baby, mine,” said Doug Burgum, the interior secretary.

It is one thing to keep eight obsolete coal plants on life support to plug immediate gaps, it is another to wager America’s AI future on this culture war banter.

It takes seven years to build a new coal plant, and longer in the US. There is a four-year waiting list for steam turbines. Nothing of scale is going to be ready before China has already run away with the AI prize.

Nuclear power is going to take even longer. “The US talks the talk on nuclear but no new reactors have actually been started,” said Chris Gadomski, BNEF’s nuclear guru and a professor at New York University.

“The Chinese build six reactors at a time on the same site. They can do it in five years and at a fifth of the price for data centres,” he said.

America has built just two reactors since 1969: the Vogtle 3 and 4 twins in Georgia – $17bn over budget and seven years late. “They were the most expensive reactors in the world until Hinkley Point and Sizewell C,” he said.

The new wave of advanced “Gen 4” small modular reactors is a good fit for data centres, but they do not yet exist in the US and they will not make much difference until the late-2030s.

What America needs is every form of power it can get its hands on this decade, whether green, brown, grey or any colour. Trump’s attempt to stop the near-completed Revolution Wind farm off New England – due to come on stream next year – tops the charts for culture war silliness, though it is hard to beat the copper tariffs.

Ditto for the Lava Ridge wind project in Idaho that was blocked in August. Ditto too for this week’s move to quash the Atlantic Shores South project off New Jersey.

Trump suspended new permits and leases on all wind projects on federal land and water in January.

His Big, Beautiful Bill was supposed to halt tax credits for wind and solar projects that produce power after 2027. But he has since gone further, subverting contracts and the intent of Congress by trying to pull forward the date in breach of a compromise with pro-renewable Republicans.

Trump has mobilised six US federal agencies to harass the renewable industry. He has lengthened the review process for all wind and solar permits. He is scaring away capital and erecting a giant sign on American soil telling clean energy funds to get lost.

Trump told the UN General Assembly last week that the Chinese were flooding the world with turbines but “barely use them” at home.

Somebody should whisper in his ear that China has installed 500GW of wind and 1,000GW of solar, nearly half the entire renewable capacity in the world.

It plans to continue rolling out renewables at a galactic scale for years to come. China is doing so because it is the cheapest and quickest way both to beat America to AI hegemony and to wean itself off seaborne fossil fuel imports forever.

It is odd that Donald Trump cannot see the looming danger to his own political brand.

Wholesale power prices have more than doubled since early 2020 in areas close to data hubs. The average price paid by households across US cities has jumped by 40pc to $0.19 a kilowatt-hour. It is ratcheting higher for structural reasons.

The cost is still less than the $0.36 or so paid in the UK, Germany and Italy – and far less than the wild spike in Europe at the outset of the Ukraine war – but the political damage may soon be comparable.

Americans consume three times as much power per capita as Britons – 12,701 kWh a year versus 4,069, according to data from Ember and the Energy Institute.

Over 20 million American households have fallen behind on their electricity bills. Rising numbers are being disconnected. We are starting to hear the same sort of stories about energy poverty that have been a staple of British headlines for four years.

Trump cannot keep blaming Joe Biden for much longer. It will soon be Maga’s energy crisis.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Building homes ‘not financially viable’ across half of England
data.description:
Building houses is not financially viable in nearly half of England, property website Zoopla has warned.

Rising costs as a result of interest rates, inflation and red tape have outpaced the growth in house prices, meaning developers in parts of the country now face a loss when building.

The cost of completing a new home has risen by 17pc since 2022, according to Zoopla, outpacing a 1pc increase in average sale prices over the same period.

Richard Donnell, executive director at Zoopla, said: “While the Government says it wants to ‘build baby build’, our analysis shows that this can only be currently achieved across half the country, in areas that are typically more expensive for consumers to buy.

“It is much harder for builders to build homes where it’s affordable for home buyers to buy.”

Viability is particularly stretched in the North. Sales prices in just 10pc of the region could cover the cost of building, Zoopla said, compared to 64pc in southern England. In County Durham, in the North East, 72pc of the area was identified as unviable, for example.

The findings are a setback for Sir Keir Starmer, who vowed to “build, baby, build” earlier this year in his push to construct 1.5 million new homes by the end of parliament.

That sentiment was echoed by Steve Reed, the new Housing Secretary, when he took over the brief following Angela Rayner’s resignation from the Government.

The Government has sought to boost building rates through planning reforms, which have included compulsory housing targets for councils and relaxing the rules for building on low-quality green belt land.

However, industry leaders have warned that the planning system is still too slow and costly. Tim Roberts, the chief executive of construction company Henry Boot, told The Telegraph earlier this week that “ridiculous” planning rules on the colour of bricks were causing huge delays to housebuilding.

Builders are also bracing for a wave of new costs from the Building Safety Levy – a tax on new residential projects to fund repairs for existing buildings – and the proposed Future Homes Standard, which may require builders to install renewable energy equipment such as solar panels and heat pumps.

Housebuilding remains at its lowest level in almost a decade, with energy performance certificates (EPC) granted for new homes falling by 8pc over the 12 months to June 2025.

A spokesman for the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government said: “Although it is difficult to interrogate analysis that has not been shared with us, the Government is leaving no stone unturned to build the 1.5 million homes people desperately need across the country.

“Fixing the archaic planning system won’t happen overnight. But because of the major planning reforms we have introduced, we will see the biggest era of housebuilding in our country’s history.

“Coupled with our £39bn investment in social and affordable housing, we will go further and faster to build baby build and restore the dream of homeownership.”

data.title: Starmer’s AI drive ‘will lead to hosepipe bans’
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer’s artificial intelligence (AI) plans risk leading to hosepipe bans and could drive up water bills, a government advisory body has warned.

A report from the Government Digital Sustainability Alliance (GDSA) said that placing major data centres in water-stressed areas could lead to “increased water bills [and] rationing”, adding it risked “potentially exacerbating social conflicts”.

Last week, the Prime Minister trumpeted a series of promised investments in AI data centres, including the construction of Britain’s most powerful AI supercomputer and a new “AI growth zone” in Northumberland.

The string of investments by US tech giants, which coincided with Donald Trump’s state visit, led to predictions of a “big bang” for the British AI industry.

However, the GDSA, a group set up by the Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs, claimed that Britain’s water supplies were not adequately prepared for a boom in data centres, which can require large quantities of water to cool computer systems.

It warned that the tech projects were a “blind spot” in official water forecasting, and that utilities were not planning for a surge in data centre infrastructure.

“The siting of water-intensive infrastructure in already water-stressed areas, particularly those with existing socio-economic vulnerabilities, raises significant environmental justice concerns,” it said.

“This practice could disproportionately impact local communities, potentially leading to increased water bills, rationing or reduced environmental flows.”

It warned there was a conflict between the Government’s AI dreams and Britain’s ability to manage water supplies.

“The rapid expansion of AI presents a profound challenge to the UK’s water security and environmental sustainability,” it said.

“The evidence demonstrates that AI’s substantial water footprint, primarily driven by data centre cooling and energy generation, is escalating at an alarming rate and is largely unaccounted for in current national water resource planning.

“This critical oversight, coupled with existing and projected water deficits in the UK, creates a significant risk of exacerbating water stress, leading to potential social and environmental conflicts.”

Source of conflict

The country is already facing pressure on its future water supply. England will require an extra five billion litres of water a day by 2050, according to the Environment Agency.

The GDSA, which is made up of business executives, academics and industry groups, exists to give the Government advice when developing new policies. Its members views do not necessarily reflect those of the Government.

The report by Chris Kenny, the co-chair of one of the GDSA’s panel groups, said measures recently proposed by water companies to improve Britain’s water supplies by 2050 did not account for the growth in data centres.

Data centres are crucial to AI and use large amounts of energy. Last week, Microsoft pledged to invest £22bn in Britain, the majority of which will go towards new infrastructure.

Thames Water warned that its water supplies risked being stressed by more than 100 new data centres planned in the regions it covers.

The tech industry has disputed that data centres risk adding to Britain’s water problems. Industry body techUK said in a report last month that the majority of new data centres used so-called closed loop-cooling systems that do not require any external water supplies.

Plans to support the AI industry have led to other conflicts within the Government.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, has championed a proposed giant data centre on Teesside that would conflict with a proposed hydrogen plant believed to be supported by Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary.

A government spokesman said: “Data centres are part of the vital infrastructure behind the digital technology that powers our businesses and everyday lives, and we need substantial investment to harness AI’s transformative potential.

“The planning system takes into account water scarcity and local plans themselves should take into account the long-term implications for water supply. We’re also unlocking £104bn in water infrastructure investment, which includes improving water supplies around data centres.”

data.title: Charity criticises ‘disturbing’ TfL video that encourages bringing cats on Tube
data.description:
A charity has criticised Transport for London (TfL) over a “disturbing” video in which it encouraged people to bring their cats on the Tube.

Cats Protection asked TfL to remove the social media video that showed cats being taken on the London Underground, on leads and released from their carrying baskets, with a caption that read: “This is your sign to start travelling with your pet around London.”

The post also showed the cats playing with toys inside a carriage, with a voiceover telling owners they can “let them relax on the floor”.

The charity called the idea “shocking”, and said that “busy stations and crowded underground platforms” would be “hugely stressful” for cats.

Nicky Trevorrow, senior behaviour manager at Cats Protection, said: “It is disturbing to see Transport for London apparently normalising passengers taking cats on the underground with a social media reel aimed at young people.

“Busy stations and crowded underground platforms with loud and sudden noises, such as trains arriving, announcements, and crowds, should be off limits for cats.

“It is shocking to think that anyone would travel on the Underground with their cat on a lead or draped on their shoulders. It is not only poor welfare, it’s unsafe.”

The TfL video advised cat owners to follow four basic rules for travelling with their pets on public transport, including going equipped with pet carriers and escape-proof harnesses, treats, water and toys.

It also told owners not to allow their cats to sit on passenger seats, but to place them inside their carriers or on the floor if kept on a lead.

The charity said it has formally contacted Transport for London asking for the video to be removed from the company’s social media channels.

Sarah Elliott, central veterinary officer at Cats Protection, added: “Restraining a cat on a lead or taking it on unnecessary journeys in backpacks can cause significant stress and inhibit a cat’s natural behaviours.

“Cats are territorial and like to roam freely, rather than go on leisure outings away from their familiar home. As well as the obvious safety and welfare concerns, cat harnesses prevent the flight response, and feeling trapped can significantly escalate stress in cats.”

A spokesman for Cats Protection said: “Promotion of cats travelling on the Tube risks encouraging more people to take an action which can be hugely stressful for cats.

“The charity advises, when a cat needs transporting, such as for a vet visit, a safe and sturdy carrier with a secure door should be used, rather than a cat backpack or collar and lead.”

TfL allows assistance dogs and “any other dog or inoffensive animal” to be taken on public transport, adding that owners “must keep it under control on a lead or in a suitable container and must not allow it on a seat”.

A TfL spokesman said: “We permit animals to travel on our network and anyone doing so should do so safely and in line with our conditions.”

data.title: Britain’s third-richest man invests $600m to prop up ‘Netflix of sports’
data.description:
Britain’s third-richest man injected a further $587m (£438m) into his streaming service Dazn last year in an effort to prop up the so-called “Netflix of sports”.

The latest investment means that Sir Leonard Blavatnik has invested more than $7bn into Dazn, which continues to rack up losses after its launch nine years ago.

In its effort to shake up the sports TV market, the streaming platform has spent vast sums buying the rights to sports such as boxing and basketball, as well as major European football matches from Germany and France.

The London-based company has also moved into betting.

But Dazn has suffered steep losses in the process, and Sir Leonard, who is worth more than £25bn, according to The Sunday Times Rich List, has been forced to prop up the business through his investment firm Access Industries.

Dazn lost a further $962m in 2024, according to newly published accounts, though this narrowed from a loss of more than $1.4bn the previous year.

That was despite raising $1bn through the sale of a roughly 5pc stake to Surj, the sports arm of Saudi Arabia’s public wealth fund, earlier this year.

The company also posted an 11pc rise in revenues to $3.2bn, which it pinned largely on subscriber growth and price rises.

Sir Leonard, a Ukrainian-born businessman, made his fortune through the privatisation of Russian oil and aluminium assets following the collapse of the Soviet Union.

Through Access Industries, he is the largest shareholder in record label giant Warner Music Group, while other investments include film studio A24 and London’s Theatre Royal Haymarket.

The 68-year-old tycoon is also a major donor to the Republican Party and has backed the “anti-woke” University of Austin, founded by journalist Bari Weiss.

Meanwhile, Dazn has strengthened its control of domestic football rights to competitions such as the Bundesliga and Ligue 1, while also securing exclusive rights to the Fifa Club World Cup in a $1bn deal.

The business has also expanded through acquisitions, including the $2.2bn takeover of Australian pay-TV operator Foxtel from Rupert Murdoch’s News Corp and telecoms group Telstra.

Dazn, led by Shay Segev as its chief executive, has set itself a target of achieving one billion global users. It has previously said it has 20 million paying subscribers, with around 1.2 billion hours streamed each year.

Staff numbers jumped by a fifth last year to more than 3,100 as the company pushed ahead with its expansion. Mr Segev’s pay jumped by more than a quarter to $5.2m.

data.title: Time is running out to save Britain’s pharma industry
data.description:
The timing could hardly have been worse. Britain’s pharmaceutical industry was already struggling with arguments over the prices that the NHS pays for its medicines, attacks on vaccines, rising competition from China and restrictions in the crucial American market.

On Friday, Donald Trump slapped 100pc tariffs on imports into the US. An industry once regarded as the jewel in the crown of the British economy is facing a crisis – and time is running out to save it.

The president’s tariff wars may have settled down across most industrial sectors. But life sciences is the one exception.

Trump announced that from the start of next month, pharmaceutical imports into the US would face 100pc tariffs unless the company making them was already building a factory in America.

The UK, which exports more than £5bn of medicines to the US every year, is likely to be especially badly hit by that, and the share prices of all the major companies in the sector were down sharply on the news.

It is not as if the industry was in great shape to start with. Lord Vallance, the science minister, argued this week that the NHS would have to increase its spending on medicines if it were to prevent an exodus of manufacturers from the UK.

The American giant Merck this month decided to scrap a £1bn investment in the UK, arguing that the health system was not doing enough to support innovation in the sector, while AstraZeneca, Britain’s largest company by market value, put a £200m investment in Cambridge on hold, following its decision to cancel an investment in Liverpool at the start of the year.

In the background, there have been a whole series of attacks on vaccines, especially from Robert F Kennedy, Trump’s controversial health secretary. And, although it is not often discussed, like many Western industries, it faces a hyper-competitive China that is starting to lead the world in innovation and technology.

China has already overtaken the United States in the number of pharma patents filed, and it is also leading in AI-driven drug discovery. If you have the patents, it will not be long before you have world-leading companies, since controlling the technology is the key to long-term success.

It will become increasingly difficult for a relatively small economy such as the UK to remain competitive. Indeed, the UK’s share of global R&D investment in healthcare has fallen from 7.3pc to 5.7pc over the last three years alone, while the UK has fallen from 4th to 10th place in the global rankings for the number of Phase III clinical trials hosted. The days when global pharma was a British/American/Swiss industry are long in the past.

That matters. The UK is no longer a world leader in many globally significant industries, but life sciences was definitely one of them. We are home to two of the global leaders – GSK and AstraZeneca – and we have a thriving industry of smaller companies and start-ups, along with the investors to back them, that feed new ideas and technologies into the pipeline.

The industry directly contributed £17bn of value added to the UK economy, according to the industry’s trade body, the ABPI, and an additional £45bn in R&D spillover revenues. There is very little else that comes close to it in its importance.

Sure, the very biggest companies may well be able to get around the president’s latest round of tariffs. The global giants already have plants in the US or can build them if necessary (indeed, AstraZeneca has already pledged to spend $50bn (£37bn) on US factories and may even move its listing to New York).

Ireland, a low-tax offshore manufacturing hub for the US giants, will be hit more severely than the UK. However, it will be far harder for smaller companies and biotech start-ups to establish themselves. After all, it is already terrifyingly expensive to develop new medicines, and adding an American factory to circumvent the tariffs will typically prove unaffordable.

Likewise, the global giants have sales all over the world and don’t depend on orders from the NHS. However, it is far harder for smaller companies and start-ups. There are very few successful global industries that do not have a thriving domestic market, and the UK no longer offers that.

That has to change. There are two big things that need to happen.

First, Britain needs to cut a deal with the Trump administration on tariffs. The president likes the UK – that much was clear from the lavish state visit this month – and we don’t have a huge surplus in medical supplies. A deal could be struck whereby any drug with global sales exceeding $1bn would be manufactured on American soil, while smaller companies would be granted tariff-free access to the US market. That would preserve the start-up culture that the industry needs to thrive.

Next, the NHS budget is always under pressure, but it has also been significantly increased over the last few years and is likely to rise again in the November Budget. Instead of spending all the cash on higher salaries and even more generous pensions for its staff, more of the money should be allocated to buying drugs. That is the only way the British industry can thrive and generate the tax revenues the NHS needs to survive.

Along with finance and arguably aerospace, the pharmaceutical sector was one of the few areas of the British economy that competed on a global stage. It generated a huge number of well-paid jobs. However, it is currently steadily slipping down the global league, and if the Government does not offer it some assistance, it may soon be too late to save it.

data.title: EU threatens Britain with ‘devastating’ tariffs
data.description:
The European Union is threatening Britain’s steel industry with tariffs of up to 50pc in a dispute that could derail Sir Keir Starmer’s attempts to reset relations with Brussels.

On Tuesday, Brussels announced plans to impose swingeing taxes on all imports coming into the bloc, amid fears that a flood of cheap steel from China and other Asian countries are crushing the Continent’s domestic industries.

British steel producers will also be hit by the crackdown, dealing a blow that risks being fatal for many companies. Almost 80pc of Britain’s steel exports currently go to the EU.

UK Steel, which represents producers, warned the new tariffs could unleash “the biggest crisis the industry has ever faced”, with steelmakers facing a potentially “devastating hit”.

Sir Keir Starmer pledged his “strong support” for the industry and said the UK was “in discussions” with the EU about the tariffs.

Government officials are now scrambling to negotiate carve-outs for Britain.

It is understood that EU officials will use the talks to pressure Britain for further concessions in the wider post-Brexit settlement, even though ministers have already given significant ground on fishing rights and youth mobility.

The Government said it was seeking “urgent clarification” on Tuesday following the announcement.

The dispute will be seen as an embarrassment for Sir Keir, who has made forging closer ties with Brussels a main pillar of his plans to boost economic growth.

In May, the Prime Minister claimed the days of political squabbling with the EU were over as he announced closer alignment with the bloc on defence, trade and agriculture. It prompted criticism that Labour was giving away too much in return for too little and that the deal would make the UK a rule-taker from Brussels. However, Sir Keir insisted it would make people better off.

The Prime Minister now faces demands for yet more concessions just months after concluding his deal. The Telegraph understands EU officials intend to use talks on steel to press Britain to allow more Europeans to enter the country under the youth mobility scheme.

The EU’s tariffs will also effectively rip up an agreement signed with the UK just two months ago to loosen quotas for construction steel.

Former business secretary Sir Jacob Rees Mogg said: “This shows that Starmer’s reset is worthless, the EU always takes its pound of flesh but never gives anything in return.”

It deals a fresh blow to the Prime Minister’s push to revitalise British industry after the country’s steel plants and car makers were hammered by Donald Trump’s trade tariffs.

Brussels’ measures have the potential to be far more damaging than President Trump’s measures. Some 78pc of Britain’s exports currently go to the EU – or around 1.9 million tonnes – compared to the 7pc which go to the US.

British firms also fear the prohibitively high EU tariffs will prompt foreign exporters to divert more of their steel to the UK, further undermining domestic industry. They are lobbying for UK tariffs in order to prevent dumping.

Peter Brennan of UK Steel said: “These tariffs would be a massive problem for British steel companies at a time when they are already under a lot of pressure.

“If we cannot get our own quotas for what the EU is doing and cannot continue to export in similar volumes, it becomes an existential problem for the industry.”

On Tuesday evening, Sir Keir Starmer said: “Our position in relation to our steel industry is one of strong support, as you saw from Scunthorpe and Port Talbot. We are in discussions with the EU about this, as we’re in discussions with the US about it.”

Brussels insisted on Tuesday that it had “no choice” but to impose the measures because of the sheer scale of cheap products flowing in from Asia.

Under the proposal, the EU will allow just 18.3 million tonnes of steel to be imported without trade tariffs, down from 28 million tonnes last year.

“Dear UK friends, you have to understand that we have no other choice than to limit the total volumes of imports that come into the EU,” a senior Commission official said.

In an echo of the tactics used by Mr Trump when he launched a trade war on allies, the Commission said countries would have to negotiate individually with the EU to secure favourable tariff-free quotas.

Even Ukraine will be forced to the table. “We’re not guaranteeing to anyone that the historical trade flows will be respected,” the official said.

Addressing talks with Britain, Maroš Šefčovič, the EU’s Brexit negotiator, told a news conference in Strasbourg: “We will negotiate these quotas in good faith as we are two very close partners.”

Asked by The Telegraph whether the proposals were hypocritical given the EU heavily criticised Mr Trump’s levies, Stéphane Séjourné, the European Commission’s (EC) vice-president, told reporters: “We won’t apologise for taking decisions for European steelmakers, to protect our markets and the jobs underlying them.”

Talks with Britain are expected in the coming weeks and Brussels is expected to demand evidence that British steel plants are not using cheap Asian imports in so-called “melt and pour” manufacturing, as well as assurances that nationalisation is not giving UK exports an unfair advantage.

The Government nationalised British Steel’s operations in Scunthorpe this year amid concerns its Chinese owners were set to close the country’s last remaining blast furnaces.

“There is nothing in our documents that suggest the UK is not dumping or not subsiding,” an EU official said.

British officials will argue that slapping fresh tariffs on exports could be considered a breach of the post-Brexit Trade and Cooperation Agreement.

The EU believe they are using powers within the pact that allow for each side to implement safeguarding measures to protect their domestic industries.

Former Tory leader Sir Iain Duncan Smith said: “This is quite clearly in breach of the free trade agreement.

“We have an arrangement with the European Union and if they just jack up sanctions, we should jack ours up against them.”

He added: “This is typical of the European Union and is a lesson to Starmer and co that at no stage should the EU ever be trusted.”

The bloc’s tariffs will deal yet another blow to the UK steel industry, which is already struggling with high electricity prices and US tariffs.

Sir Keir claimed to have negotiated US tariffs down to zero but was subsequently forced to admit this was a premature promise. So far, he has failed to negotiate zero-rate tariffs.

Andrew Griffiths, the Conservative shadow business secretary, said: “The Government’s lack of a real plan for steel is unravelling fast.

“From the dead-on-arrival zero US steel tariffs to this significant new threat from the EU, it’s clear ministers are flailing as the industry threatens to collapse around them.”

Responding to the EU’s announcement on Tuesday, Chris McDonald, the industry minister, said: “We will always defend our critical steel industry which is why we are pushing the EC for urgent clarification of the impact of this move on the UK.

“It’s vital we protect trade flows between the UK and EU and we will work with our closest allies to address global challenges rather than adding to our industries’ woes.”

The Government has claimed its wider Brexit reset will deliver a £9bn boost to the British economy.

data.title: GSK chief to step down after nine years in charge
data.description:
Dame Emma Walmsley will step down as GSK’s chief executive at the end of the year, concluding nine years at the helm of Britain’s second-largest pharmaceuticals company.

The £62bn FTSE giant announced on Monday that Dame Emma will be replaced by Luke Miels, the chief commercial officer, who joined in 2017 and is responsible for overseeing vaccines and medicines globally.

He will take charge of the business in January, at a time when the industry is under pressure from Donald Trump.

The US president has threatened 100pc tariffs on drugmakers if they aren’t investing in America.

Dame Emma, who was the first woman to run a major pharmaceutical company when she became boss of GSK in 2017, is stepping down after struggling to boost the company’s share price.

Since her appointment, the company’s valuation has fallen by roughly 10pc. GSK shares rose by more than 3pc following the announcement.

Dame Emma said: “2026 is a pivotal year for GSK to define its path for the decade ahead and I believe the right moment for new leadership.

“As CEO, you hope to leave the company you love stronger than you found it and prepare for seamless succession.”

During her time as chief executive, Dame Emma spun off GSK’s consumer brands division to create Haleon, listing the business on the London Stock Exchange after rejecting a £50bn offer from Unilever in 2022.

This followed heightened scrutiny over the company’s strategy as critics claimed it lacked direction. At the time, GSK was selling products ranging from Sensodyne toothpaste to hepatitis vaccines.

Since then, analysts have claimed the drugmaker has struggled to develop an exciting pipeline of new medicines, something Dame Emma said was her biggest priority when taking charge.

In particular, investors are concerned that GSK has made no headway in fast-growing areas like obesity drugs, while being overly reliant on vaccines where demand is weakening.

Dame Emma is one of London’s highest-paid chief executives, commanding a pay package of £10.6m last year. This was down from £12.7m the year prior.

Sir Jonathan Symonds, the GSK chairman, said: “On behalf of the board, I want to thank Emma and acknowledge her outstanding leadership in delivering a strategic transformation of GSK, including the successful demerger of Haleon.

“GSK today is necessarily very different to the company she was appointed to nine years ago and has a bright and ambitious future.”

data.title: ‘Auction house sold my 90-year-old parents’ valuables for £26,000 and then went bust’
data.description:
Has a company treated you unfairly? Our Consumer Champion is available to help. For how to contact her, click here.

Dear Katie,

A couple of years ago, I had to move both my parents, who are both in their 90s, into a care home because they were no longer able to look after themselves.

My father died in March this year, and my mother, although still alive, has dementia, so I started proceedings to sell their flat and all their possessions, which they no longer had any use for.

The flat was sold in May, and I sent their possessions to a well-known local auction house called Aldridges of Bath. Their valuables – which included a family heirloom 3.5-carat diamond ring, various Victorian pieces of furniture and silverware – were sold in five auctions across May, June, and July, fetching more than £26,000.

However, it is now September, and neither me or my mother have been paid a penny from the auction house.

I have chased it multiple times. When I get through, I am told my case is with a director, but he is never available to speak to me. When I look at Aldridges of Bath’s website, I see there are no upcoming auctions organised, which seems unusual.

I have approached a local solicitor who has sent a letter to Aldridges, but there has been no response.

I fear Aldridges of Bath may have gone under, meaning we may never see our money again.

As you can imagine, this has been a very stressful experience. I was entrusted to sell my parents’ worldly valuables, however it looks like things have gone very wrong.

Could you possibly work your magic on this?

–Anon

Dear reader,

I was very sorry to hear about the death of your father and your mother’s mental decline, leaving you responsible for dealing with all their financial and other affairs. It hasn’t been an easy task for you, and that’s without this auction house, Aldridges of Bath, failing to pay up.

At around the same time that I started looking into what had gone wrong here, Aldridges updated its website with a notice stating that it had taken the unfortunate decision to cease trading, advising people to contact its liquidator, Begbies Traynor, instead.

The website has now disappeared from the internet altogether, and the domain is up for sale.

Insolvency documents indicate that the company has assets of approximately £50,000 and liabilities of around £200,000, with many other creditors being customers who were awaiting payment following the auctioning of their belongings.

I’m afraid, as I think you’ve already gathered, all of this is terrible news for the prospect of your mother ever receiving the full £26,000 she is owed.

In situations such as this, liquidators are appointed to fairly distribute the remaining assets among creditors, and I’m afraid it appears that you have no more rights to the money than anyone else. I’m sorry to say that this means you’re unlikely to receive more than a tiny fraction of the money you are owed here. Under the circumstances, I understand that this will be deeply upsetting.

However, since the auctions in question were held in May, June and July, and Aldridges did not go into administration until late August, I wanted to know why you didn’t receive any money from the sale of your parents’ possessions – despite your contract stating that you would receive payment within 28 days of the auction.

Many auction houses hold so-called “client money” separately from their other funds to prevent it from being mixed with other funds and used for non-client expenses. However, I read in Aldridge’s insolvency documents that client money was not held in a separate account.

This, along with the timing of events, leads me to wonder where your mother’s £26,000, along with money owed to other customers, has gone.

I felt this man, who’d been at the helm of Aldridges, had some questions to answer over the whereabouts of your money, so I sent a reporter to his house to see what he had to say for himself. However, when they arrived, there was no one home and no car on the drive, and my request for contact has so far received no response.

It will now be up to the liquidator to assess the goings-on at Aldridges and determine where creditors’ money went. It will then submit a confidential report to the Government on the directors’ conduct, which will include questions around the ring-fencing of funds and why the auctions continued.

If it finds anything as part of its investigation, it could open the door to the directors’ personal assets being seized to pay creditors. However, even if this did transpire, it could be a long and ultimately fruitless road towards recompense.

I know you were hoping I could “work my magic” on your case, and I truly wish there was more I could do in this regard. But for now, we need to let the professional liquidators do their work and await the outcome.

I know you are partly blaming yourself, but please try not to.

data.title: M&S accused of misleading shoppers with bread ingredients
data.description:
Marks & Spencer has been accused of misleading shoppers about the ingredients in its own-brand loaves.

The Real Bread Campaign claimed it had forced M&S into a review of its marketing and labelling after making complaints to Trading Standards earlier this year.

The retailer said it “continually work[s] ... to ensure our labelling is clear for our customers”.

The campaign group took issue with several claims including the assurance that one of its white sliced loaves only contained four ingredients when it actually had 11. A sunflower and spelt loaf featured a claim on front of the packet that it had six ingredients when it had 13.

The group also challenged M&S for claiming that bags of flour aimed at home bakers were made from “an ancient variety of wheat” when the flour was in fact made from a modern variety of rye.

Chris Young, the coordinator of the Real Bread Campaign, claimed that such assertions were “clearly factually incorrect” and added: “We want companies to be open and honest in their marketing and we want the Government to ensure that companies are open and honest.”

A spokesman for M&S said: “All our packaging is legally compliant and we continually work with our primary authority to ensure our labelling is clear for our customers.

“Our Only Ingredients range has been created for customers who are looking for products with fewer ingredients and we’re thrilled with the positive response we have seen from customers.

“We develop all our foods using processes as close to the kitchen as possible and with ingredients you’d typically find on your cupboard shelves. This approach contributes to the high-quality customers expect from M&S.”

Freshly baked complaints

The row comes as the retailer ramps up its food offerings across the country. M&S is opening six new food halls in London while renovating and expanding an existing seven sites.

The work will add more than 70,000sq ft to the retailer’s London space, including larger bakeries.

At the same time, M&S is opening food halls in 12 former Homebase sites across the South of England. These will be their biggest food halls to date. Its food hall in Godalming, Surrey, will be 22,000sq ft and is set to open by mid-2026.

The Real Bread Campaign has raised similar grievances in the past, including taking issue with M&S’s allegedly false claim that its bread is “freshly baked in store”.

The campaign claims that all M&S bread is in fact manufactured elsewhere and merely reheated in store using what Mr Young called “loaf tanning salons”.

A spokesman for M&S said: “We have a huge selection available in our in-store bakeries which are baked to perfection by our expertly trained team.”

M&S is far from being the only alleged offender. Sainsbury’s and Lidl last year dropped their “freshly baked” in store boasts after similar claims about inaccuracy.

Mr Young called for the Government to introduce “an Honest Crust Act of updated and improved composition, labelling and marketing standards”. He said clear labelling was important “so that people have the chance to make better-informed choices”.

The Food Standards Agency until recently provided guidance on the use of the terms “fresh”, “pure” and “natural” in labelling – but Mr Young said the quango had quietly dropped the advice earlier this year.

Both the Real Bread Campaign and the Chartered Trading Standards Institute called for its reinstatement.

The Real Brand Campaign was founded in 2008 to promote what it calls “genuine” bread made without chemical raising agents, preservatives or any other additives – typically meaning sourdough.

The Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs was contacted for comment.

data.title: Aston Martin warns of disruption in wake of JLR cyber attack
data.description:
Aston Martin has said it risks being hit by the fallout of a cyber attack on Jaguar Land Rover (JLR) as it issued a profit warning and cut its sales forecasts.

The carmaker told investors it was braced for “increased potential for supply chain pressures, particularly following the recent cyber incident at a major UK automotive manufacturer”.

The cyber attack on JLR, which has shut down production of Range Rover and Jaguar vehicles, has piled pressure on a vast network of automotive suppliers across Britain.

JLR shares some critical parts suppliers with fellow carmakers including Aston Martin, Bentley and McLaren. These small businesses have been grappling with late or missed payments and some have been forced to lay off hundreds of staff.

Some suppliers have warned they face a looming cash crunch from the shutdown. Last week, the Government confirmed the taxpayer would underwrite a £1.5bn bank loan to JLR intended to accelerate invoice payments to affected businesses.

However, The Sunday Times reported that the final rescue package was yet to be signed off, prompting fresh concerns from JLR’s suppliers.

The warning from Aston Martin comes despite JLR restarting some engine manufacturing on Monday at its factory in Wolverhampton more than a month after its production lines were shut down by a cyber attack.

However, large parts of JLR’s supply chain remain at a standstill as it attempts to restart its IT systems following the hack on Aug 31.

On Monday, Aston Martin said expected deliveries of its new £850,000 Valhalla hybrid supercar would be below previous forecasts, at around 150 this year.

It blamed the delays on final regulatory approvals and flagged the uncertainty of the US government shutdown.

The Vantage-maker also cut its overall expected sales for 2025, warning it faced potential disruption from US tariffs, new taxes in China and pressure on its suppliers from the JLR cyber attack.

The luxury carmaker added it had launched a review of its upcoming capital expenditure and that it expected to lower investment in engineering over the next four years.

“An immediate review of future cost and capital expenditures has been initiated by the management team”, Aston Martin said. “This will also include a review of the future product cycle plan in response to market and regulatory dynamics.”

Aston Martin has been forced into a series of sales revisions and profit warnings as Donald Trump’s tariffs and vehicle quotas hit British car exports.

It said it delivered 1,430 vehicles in the third quarter of 2025, below expectations and lower than last year, when it sold 1,641 cars. It also cut estimates for full-year earnings before interest and taxes to a £110m loss.

data.title: ‘Dad ordered new curtains before he died but the company still wants payment’
data.description:
Do you have a legal question to put to Gary? Email askalawyer@telegraph.co.uk or use the form at the bottom of the page.

Dear Gary

My stepfather died a few months ago. Before he died, he placed an order with a local firm for fitted curtains. The contract was for supply and fit, and he paid a 50pc deposit. However, he died a few weeks later.

Shortly after his death, the company made contact to arrange a fitting. They were informed of his death, but are still requesting full payment. Do they have a valid claim on the estate?

– D, via email

Dear D,

I am sorry for your bereavement. Your question touches on several legal principles, including contract law, consumer rights, and the duty of those administering a deceased person’s estate to settle their liabilities before distributing the estate to beneficiaries.

Whether the cost of the fitted curtains ordered by your stepfather before his death constitutes a valid claim on his estate depends, in the first place, on what the contract he entered into states. It also clarifies what was delivered and what the law says about cancelling such contracts. The issue of how the law treats such “bespoke goods” is also relevant.

In general, contracts entered into during a person’s lifetime do not automatically end when they die. In other words, contractual obligations often survive death, unless expressly stated otherwise. This means that the individuals handling the estate (the executors, if a valid will exists, or administrators, if one does not) assume responsibility for settling the deceased’s debts and obligations using the estate’s funds. This includes debts arising from valid contracts.

So, the starting point is that, yes, the curtain firm could have a claim against the estate. But whether that claim is enforceable depends on other factors.

One very important factor is whether goods are bespoke or not. Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015, consumers are granted the right to cancel contracts for goods and services made off-premises or at a distance (for example, ordered by phone or online) within 14 days of the contract being made.

However, there is an exception to this general rule under section 28(1)(b) of the Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013. If the goods are made to the consumer’s specifications or are clearly personalised, which is often the case with fitted curtains, then the consumer does not have the right to cancel the contract simply because they change their mind. Or if they unfortunately die.

This exception exists because bespoke goods are typically not resalable. Therefore, if your stepfather ordered made-to-measure curtains, then the company may have a right to recover the cost of producing them, even if installation did not take place.

You do mention the contract was for “supply and fit”. This is significant. In a contract of this kind, payment in full may not become due until both parts of the contract have been completed, that is, until the curtains have been supplied and installed.

If the curtain firm had not yet delivered or fitted the curtains at the time of death, and especially if fitting was essential to the overall value of the service, the company may not be entitled to demand full payment.

However, as I say, they may still have a right to be compensated for costs already incurred, that is, the making of the curtains, particularly if they were bespoke. And in fairness, the making of the curtains is probably the major cost, as compared to the fitting.

Even in cases where the customer is deceased, the supplier has a duty to “mitigate loss”. This means taking reasonable steps to avoid unnecessary financial loss. If the curtain firm could, for instance, reuse some of the materials or reallocate the product, their claim should be reduced accordingly.

It is not enough for them to simply demand full payment without showing what steps they took to mitigate any loss or what proportion of the goods or services was delivered.

The person dealing with the estate of a deceased person is legally responsible for assessing and paying valid debts from estate assets. Accordingly, in this situation, I would request three things: a copy of the contract or order form, a breakdown of the work done or costs incurred and confirmation of whether the goods were bespoke and whether fitting was attempted or scheduled.

Only when that information is provided should the estate consider making any payment. And then, only to the extent that the firm has a legitimate and proportionate claim.

Remember also that the person administering the estate has a duty to the beneficiaries to preserve the estate, and the beneficiaries may complain if they feel a debt has been paid that should not have been.

So, whatever you do, explain to all concerned what is happening, who is being paid and why they are being paid. Another example of how being an executor is an onerous task!

In summary, it is likely that the curtain company may have a valid claim against the estate if the curtains were made to measure and the firm can demonstrate that it incurred costs that cannot be recovered elsewhere. However, it is not automatically entitled to full payment simply because a contract was signed.

The company’s claim must be based on what has actually been supplied or spent and balanced against the fact that the contract was not fully performed. While the Consumer Rights Act 2015 and related regulations limit cancellation rights in the case of bespoke goods, the legislation also requires the supplier of goods to act reasonably.

I therefore suggest that you find out more and make an informed judgment. Indeed, I hope that you act in a reasonable manner towards the curtain firm (which I assume is a small business). I hope they will treat you in the same manner.

Ask a Lawyer should not be taken as formal legal advice, but rather as a starting point for readers to undertake their own further research.
