data.title: Viewers are the reason TV is so bad
data.description:
Television is not what it used to be. In The Telegraph last week former producer David Herman blamed TV executives, governors and ministers, who he called “spineless, middlebrow and clueless”.

Well, as a former TV executive, I’m sorry to say it’s not our fault. The real power in our industry is wielded not by us, but by the viewer.

We in the industry do not dream of making endless programmes of people “bonking on desert islands”. We’re as tired of them as anyone else. But we do not have the luxury of dismissing the viewer and supplying them with arts documentaries instead.

Forty years ago, TV execs were in the glorious position of barely needing to care about the viewer at all. No matter what they put out, millions watched because they had no choice. Whether it was a 25-part series on ancient Mesopotamia or quadratics-solving lectures on the Open University, the masses would tune in. Even the Test Card famously drew an audience.

But now, viewers can go elsewhere if they don’t like what we serve them. And increasingly, that’s exactly what they do, turning not to rival channels, but to rival mediums that threaten the existence of television altogether. The cheap, user-generated online digital content on YouTube and TikTok superficially resembles TV because it is watched on a screen, but it’s as far removed from TV as books are.

Television is sophisticated. It is long-form narratives that unfold over an hour, with beginnings, middles and ends. That’s as true of The Chase or The Great British Bake Off as it is of Succession and Slow Horses. The problem is that viewers are unwilling, or unable, to pay attention that long.

Year on year, overall viewing of television is falling. Instead of concentrating on something for an hour, a growing number of viewers prefer to doomscroll through a blizzard of electrifying but minuscule clips on TikTok, or flick non-committally around the timelines of vastly less demanding YouTube content.

It’s a trend that will bankrupt the commercial channels eventually if it can’t be halted, which is depressing if you want long-form storytelling to be part of the popular culture. It is already causing lay-offs and cuts. And it makes the need to respect viewer taste unavoidable. Commissioning programmes they aren’t interested in will only hasten the demise of television itself.

There are still audiences for highbrow subjects, but they are far too small to sustain a commercial TV channel. Sky Arts and BBC4 are reputational, not profitable. This is why higher-brow content is flourishing online instead on podcasts like The Rest Is History, which have vastly lower running costs.

That doesn’t mean TV has to throw in the towel on Lord Reith’s famous ambition to “inform, educate and entertain”. Most TV execs still aspire to do that no less than they ever did. But it does mean rethinking how to do it.

And so, yes, sometimes that might even mean delivering your educational messages via romantic encounters on a desert island, as Channel 4’s Virgin Island did recently. Look beneath the sensationalist title and setting, which helped it to become one of the biggest streaming hits of the year for Channel 4, and you will find a sensitive and tender exploration of important, complex issues. Were he alive today, I hope Lord Reith would approve.

But maybe, as many do, you still think TV has gone to the dogs and want someone to blame. Well, I’m afraid you must blame yourselves, dear viewers. De Tocqueville observed that democracies “get the leaders we deserve”. So too, the television.

Alf Lawrie is a TV executive and former Channel 4 commissioner

data.title: Countryside that inspired Thomas Hardy under threat
data.description:
The countryside that inspired Thomas Hardy is under threat from a housing development.

Dorset council has been criticised over proposals to build 4,000 homes on land that influenced the Victorian author’s novels.

The local authority is under pressure to build more than 30,000 new homes in the county by 2036.

However, campaigners opposing the move claim it would be “pure sacrilege” to destroy the water meadows and floodplains.

Protesters from the campaign group Save the Area North of Dorchester (Stand) carried banners saying “build the houses we need, not houses for greed” and “save our countryside” as they marched from the centre of Dorchester to the edge of the proposed site.

Critics also include Lord Fellowes of West Stafford, the author of Downton Abbey, who lives near the area, as well as the Thomas Hardy Society and the Campaign to Protect Rural England.

Mark Chutter, the chairman of the Hardy Society, said: “We are not against affordable housing, but this site is just wrong.

“To build on the ancient water meadows and floodplains would be pure sacrilege. Tourism in the two relies on our links with Hardy.”

Dorset council said the build would be high-quality and “deliver a beautiful and thriving place”, calling it North Dorchester Garden Community.

Mr Chutter claimed it would become a “concrete jungle”, adding: “With our NHS bursting at the seams and our GP surgeries inundated, alongside the traffic within the town adding vastly to pollution, how will this development survive?”

Jane Ashdown, the chairman of Stand, added that locals against this development were not Nimbys but wanted housing built that met local needs.

“It wouldn’t address the needs of local young families and key workers who would be priced out of such a car-dependent development,” she said.

Lord Fellowes, who is the president of the Hardy Society, previously spoke out against the plans.

“There are very few other world renowned novelists where you still have the house they were born in, the house they wrote their books in and the villages and countryside they based their characters and scenes on,” he said.

Hardy’s birthplace in Stinsford borders the land with planned works for housing by the Dorset council.

The author regularly walked across the landscape which inspired his writing, in particular the 1886 novel The Mayor of Casterbridge.

The deadline for consultation with the Dorset council is Oct 13. The North Dorchester Garden Consortium said Hardy’s works heavily influenced its plans and will submit an application for the site.

If approved, the first phase is set to begin in 2028 and completed by 2034 with the whole site built by 2048.

The Telegraph has approached Dorset council for comment.

data.title: Best cash Isas: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
A cash Isa is similar to a savings account, but with a crucial difference – you don’t pay tax on the interest you earn. It is one of four types of adult individual savings accounts (Isas) – the others are stocks and shares Isas, innovative finance Isas and lifetime Isas.

You can currently save up to £20,000 per tax year, which is known as the Isa allowance. Your Isa allowance is renewed at the start of the tax year on April 6, and you cannot carry over any unused allowance from the previous year – if you don’t use it, you lose it.

You can either deposit this sum into one account or spread it across several, and you can transfer your Isa savings between providers if you want to take advantage of a better rate elsewhere.

In this guide, Telegraph Money explains how cash Isas work, covering:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best cash Isas in October
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa
  • Why choose a cash Isa?
  • Cash Isa FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables below show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best cash Isas in October

Find the best cash Isa for you using the tables below.

Best easy-access cash Isas

These accounts usually allow you to make as many deposits and withdrawals as you like – but this flexibility can come with lower rates. For the savings equivalent, you can see our guide to the best easy-access savings accounts.

Best variable rate cash Isas

Accounts in the table below show the best Isa rates on the market for accounts that offer a variable rate of interest. While this could increase at any time – which can be valuable when rates are on an upward trend – it could also decrease.

Best notice cash Isas

These accounts require you to give a certain amount of notice to your provider when you want to make a withdrawal, indicating the number of days you must wait until your money is released. You can usually make as many withdrawals as you like – but be sure to check the account terms first.

If this way of saving suits you, make sure you check our guide to the best notice accounts for alternative options.

Best fixed-rate cash Isas

These types of Isas require you to lock your money away for a specified period of time. The rate of interest is guaranteed throughout this period, which can give you peace of mind should other rates take a tumble.

For the savings account equivalent, which you might want to use if you’ve used up your Isa allowance, see our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

For other types of tax-free saving, see our guide to the best Junior cash Isas for under-18s. Or, if you’re saving to buy a home or fund your retirement, don’t miss our guide to the best lifetime Isas.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa

Choosing the best cash Isa for you can be tricky, as it depends on why you’re saving and how you want to save – but there should be options to suit everyone.

Caitlyn Eastell, of analyst Moneyfactscompare, said: “For savers that are more tax savvy, cash Isas may be the ideal option as you receive a £20,000 Isa allowance each year and can pay into multiple Isas within the same period providing this limit is not exceeded. Isas aren’t too dissimilar to their traditional savings counterparts with easy access, notice, fixed and regular accounts being an option.

“It is worth considering that for the variable rate options, interest could increase or decrease over time, and those Isas that are fixed for an agreed term will either not allow earlier access or may deduct a certain amount of interest.”

Cash Isas can also be an effective way of saving for the longer term.

Mark Hicks, of Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Cash Isas have certainly seen a resurgence of late. Looking at what may be coming in Labour’s Budget, and the rhetoric around higher taxes, cash Isas should be the first port of call for all savers at the moment.

“If you’ve got £20,000 or less, definitely go for a cash Isa first and ensure your money is protected from tax. If you’re fortunate enough to have more than £20,000 to save in an Isa, then you can look at other saving accounts as well.”

Why choose a cash Isa?

Cash Isas are beneficial for many savers as they offer an easy way to make sure your returns remain free from tax.

Their popularity took a dip when the personal savings allowance (PSA) was introduced in 2016, as it means some savers can earn up to £1,000 in tax-free savings interest each tax year. When interest rates are low, only those with large savings pots or high incomes come into the scope of paying tax on savings interest.

However, savings rates have been high for some time; until recently it was not uncommon to be earning 5pc. Someone with a £1,000 PSA could face a savings tax bill with £20,000 in savings – a problem that doesn’t occur with a cash Isa. What’s more, your growth is protected as it compounds for years into the future, which has helped some savers become Isa millionaires.

The main issue that comes with a cash Isa is the £20,000 Isa limit on how much you can pay into it each tax year. If you want to move a large savings deposit in order to protect your returns from tax, it may need to be moved gradually over several years.

In some cases, interest rates offered by cash Isas can be lower than their savings equivalent. Providers are well aware of the extra tax benefit Isas offer, and know they don’t have to be as competitive with rates.

It’s important to shop around and find the most competitive rate you can. Cash Isa rates can be lower than their savings account counterparts, but the tax-free benefits of an Isa will often outweigh the loss of a little interest.

If you’re not sure whether you could be in danger of having to pay tax on your savings interest, try the Telegraph Money savings tax calculator – simply enter your savings, interest rate and tax band and it can tell you whether you’d be better off getting an Isa.

Cash Isa FAQs

How many cash Isas can I have?

There is no limit to the number of cash Isas you can have – but it’s a good idea not to have so many that you lose track of them.

You can hold cash Isas you’ve paid into in past tax years, and – now that Isa rules have changed – you can also open and pay into as many as you like in the same tax year. The key thing is to make sure you don’t pay in more than £20,000 across all Isas in the same tax year.

Can you combine multiple Isas into one?

Yes, you can combine Isas into one account – but in order to do this without affecting the tax status of the money, or your Isa allowance, you must move the cash via an Isa transfer.

There are quite a few rules to bear in mind when you do this – you can find out more in our comprehensive guide to Isa transfers.

Would a cash Isa beat inflation?

This depends on the rate you’re earning and the rate of inflation. At the time of writing, lots of cash Isas can beat inflation, but you increasingly need to shop around for them as rates continue to slide. It’s a good idea to keep an eye on what’s happening with inflation versus the best Isa rates so you can gauge whether or not you have a competitive deal.

Our inflation calculator shows the effect rising prices can have on your savings.

Is it too late to put money in an Isa?

It’s never too late to put money in an Isa to benefit from its tax-free status. However, you may miss the end of the tax year if you leave it too late to make a deposit.

The annual Isa allowance resets at the start of a tax year on April 6. You can put all of this into one account, or split it among several.

Mr Hicks said: “Isas were typically seasonal, with a big spike in demand around the end of the tax year. They are much less timely now and every year the cash Isa season gets longer and longer.”

data.title: Falling property prices spell trouble in the Magic City
data.description:
US presidential libraries are usually based in the honouree’s home state.

The William J Clinton Library and Museum is in Little Rock, Arkansas. Texas has no fewer than three presidential libraries, housing documents and correspondence from both Bushes and Lyndon B Johnson.

Yet as he often does, Donald Trump is eschewing tradition. The president is turning his back on his home state of New York and has instead picked Florida for his future library.

The president’s son Eric has promised to construct “one of the most beautiful buildings ever built” after Florida Governor Ron DeSantis donated a 2.6-acre patch of prime land in downtown Miami to Trump’s non-profit foundation.

Arguably no state has come to symbolise the Trump presidency more than Florida, with billionaires and politicians alike visiting his “winter White House” Mar-a-Lago, in Palm Beach.

The property market in Miami – just an hour’s drive down the coast from the president’s resort – has flourished in recent years, in no small part thanks to Trump’s embrace.

Jeff Bezos, the Amazon tycoon who has made moves to curry favour with the president, has bought multiple properties on Indian Creek, a Miami island nicknamed “billionaire bunker”, since Trump’s re-election.

Yet experts now fear that the property market may be approaching a bust.

Miami has the highest risk of a property downturn out of 21 major cities across the world, according to UBS. It is the second year in a row that the city has topped the investment bank’s real estate bubble index.

An affordability squeeze for the middle class, onerous new property regulations and worsening hurricanes in the area have all put the Magic City at risk of a slump.

Mad dash to Miami

The patchy housing market is just one sign of wider uncertainty about the outlook of the US economy.

Oxford Economics last week warned that “risks are swirling around the economy”. Deutsche Bank has said “the US would be close to or in recession this year” without the recent surge in artificial intelligence (AI) spending. Concerns about a stock market bubble fuelled by this AI investment are also growing.

The US Federal Reserve warned at its most recent meeting that growth had moderated this year and could slow to 1.6pc for 2025, down from 2.8pc last year.

While house prices are climbing in cities like New York, Chicago, Cleveland and Boston, America’s sun belt is dragging the overall market down. The national Case-Shiller index of house prices has now declined for five months in a row. A 1.8pc annual drop in July was the largest in two years.

It is a stark change from just a few years ago. Miami’s property market boomed during the pandemic, which brought an unprecedented wave of relocations from across the US.

“There was a mad dash to Miami,” says Miltiadis Kastanis, executive director of sales at Compass real estate agents in Miami. “It was a frenzy, multiple offers, money was to be made.”

Cash flooded in as the likes of Ken Griffin, the founder of Citadel, and Paul Singer, the boss of Elliott Investment Management, relocated their hedge funds in pursuit of sunny weather and lower taxes.

House prices have climbed by roughly 50pc in real terms over the last five years – but surging prices have driven away locals who can now no longer afford the city.

“Obviously Miami became expensive, really expensive,” says David Siddons, a Miami real estate agent who sells properties above $1.5m (£1.1m). “That created a force among people who weren’t making great money to migrate out.”

An affordability squeeze partly stoked by higher demand has been dramatically compounded by the fact that average mortgage rates have more than doubled from 2.88pc to 6.34pc over the last five years. This has stemmed the flow of people looking to relocate to Miami.

Edgar Escorcia, a real estate agent who typically sells homes in the $800,000 to $1m range, says demand in the corner of the market he works in is down by a third compared to pre-pandemic levels.

“Florida is where a lot of the weakness is concentrated right now in the US,” says Chen Zhao, head of economics research at Redfin.

Sellers now outnumber buyers by more than 100pc in Miami, Zhao says, compared to the national average of 35pc.

Realtor.com figures show that Miami has 9.7 months of housing inventory. That means that it would take nearly 10 months to sell all listed properties in the city at the current pace of sales and assuming that no other properties came to the market.

It is the biggest glut of supply in the country.

Price pains and hurricanes

This imbalance between supply and demand has begun to chip away at values. Sale prices fell by 1.3pc in July compared to a year earlier, according to the S&P Case-Shiller index.

Analysts are not expecting a dramatic crash of the kind seen in 2008 when there were waves of home foreclosures. However, prices are expected to fall – and drops could be significant.

“Maybe we’ll see prices come down by 5pc or so,” says Zhao.

Escorcia thinks the decline could be as much as 10pc over the next two years.

The median listing price in Miami has already dropped at the steepest pace in the country since 2022.

This in part reflects the fact that sellers in Miami are taking their properties off the market at the fastest pace in the country amid a glut of supply.

At the top end of the market, agents say the money is still flowing. In 2022, Siddons’ team helped 154 families relocate to Miami. This year the number will be just 40 or 50, he says – but this is still quadruple the number in a typical year before Covid.

Cash buyers and international demand are still bolstering the $5m-plus market, says Kastanis. “These are buyers who follow the stock market and not the interest rate market.”

Further down the housing ladder, however, there are signs that the bottom may be beginning to fall out.

Half-a-million-dollar condos have become “harder to sell than a $30m condo on the ocean”, Kastanis says.

The market for apartments has been hardest hit. As well as affordability concerns, new building safety regulations introduced in the wake of the 2021 collapse of a building in Miami’s Surfside neighbourhood have depressed the market for older flats.

New rules require buildings to hold substantial cash reserves for maintenance and to complete mandated repairs after inspections.

This added cost has caused a slump in demand for housing in buildings that are more than 10 years old in the likes of Mid Beach, South Beach and Downtown Miami, says Kastanis.

Adding to woes is the worsening weather.

Florida’s hurricane season – which runs from June to November – has become increasingly intense, driving insurance premiums to the highest level in the country.

Some homes in flood zones have become uninsurable, with selling prices slashed to just the value of the land according to Siddons.

It is this constellation of woes that has prompted UBS to predict a slump. So far, the market has managed to avoid a nasty crash, instead suffering something like a slow puncture – but estate agents working there worry just how long this can continue.

Eric Trump has promised that his father’s presidential library will become “an icon on the Miami skyline”.

It risks also becoming a memorial to a frothy property market on the cusp of a painful slump.

data.title: Salt Bae suffers record loss as diners lose appetite for £600 steaks
data.description:
Social media chef Salt Bae’s restaurant business slumped to a record loss last year after US expansion went awry.

Nusr-et UK, which owns the Knightsbridge restaurant Nusr-Et, lost £5.4m before tax last year, according to newly filed accounts. It came after the business wrote down the value of its US operations by £6.6m.

Diners at Nusr-Et can choose a wagyu strip loin for £680, an Istanbul steak for £240 and a skirt steak for as much as £380, followed by a 24-carat gold-plated baklava for £50.

Sales at the Knightsbridge restaurant, owned by Turkish chef Nusret Gökçe, nicknamed Salt Bae, rose by £1m to £10m last year.

But overall the UK company slumped to a loss after American expansion struggled.

Nusret US Inc, which is fully owned by the UK business, was forced to shut one of two New York restaurants and its Boston outpost last year.

In June of this year, Salt Bae closed several other US locations, including its Beverly Hills branch, as part of a “realignment to focus on international growth.” The chef has now gone from having seven locations in the US to just two in the US: in New York’s midtown and Miami’s financial district.

“They tried some markets out,” a spokesman for Nusr-Et restaurant group said last year. “Some worked while others haven’t.”

The loss of £5.5m for the UK company compared to a profit of £1.7m a year earlier. That figure had almost halved from 2022.

Mr Gökçe found fame in 2017 when a video of him flamboyantly seasoning a steak went viral.

He went on to start his own line of restaurants with eleven locations across the world, including in Milan, Mykonos, Istanbul and Abu Dhabi, with a London branch opening in September 2021.

The social media star now has over 52 million Instagram followers, and his London restaurant has hosted David Beckham, Cristiano Ronaldo and Wayne Rooney.

However, Mr Gökçe has proved a divisive figure. He enraged football fans on social media after finding his way onto the pitch at the Fifa World Cup final in 2022, where he was photographed shaking hands with Lionel Messi and handling the trophy following Argentina’s win.

His Knightsbridge outpost is also ranked 14,912 out of 22,655 London restaurants on Tripadvisor, with a rating of 2.9 stars out of five from almost 400 reviews.

Nusret UK declined to comment.

data.title: Mirror journalists poised to strike over mass job cuts
data.description:
Journalists at The Mirror are poised to strike after they warned artificial intelligence (AI) was behind a looming round of job cuts.

The National Union of Journalists (NUJ) is balloting members over industrial action after publisher Reach last month said it would make hundreds of journalists redundant.

The union raised concerns about unreasonable workloads for remaining staff members after years of gruelling cuts, as well as the growing use of AI.

Reach has developed its own AI chatbot, dubbed Guten, aimed at helping reporters to quickly rewrite press releases, wire copy and stories from rival publishers.

The NUJ accused the company of using the technology to replace journalists, adding that it was harming the quality of output and blurring the distinctions between the group’s different titles.

Laura Davison, the NUJ general secretary, said: “These proposals, if carried out in full, will mean the dismissal of experienced and highly professional journalists. But it will also place considerable burdens on those who remain and newsrooms will be made weaker and less able to produce quality journalism.

“While the company insists that AI is not behind the cuts, this assertion is clearly not accepted by our members and is not borne out by the evidence. The union believes that left unchecked, these savage cuts will not stop here to the detriment of what we all understand as journalism.

“The hollowing out of newsroom staffing in favour of AI chatter overseen by shrinkingly small staffing is the road to nowhere.”

Reach, which owns The Mirror and Express as well as scores of regional titles, last month announced plans to slash 321 jobs including 186 in its editorial division. While the company will also create 135 new roles, predominantly in audio and video production, roughly 600 roles are at risk in total.

Staff at The Mirror will be particularly affected by the cuts with 40 writers and editors expected to lose their jobs. Members took a unanimous decision to be balloted for strike action at a recent meeting.

The NUJ will also ballot members at Reach’s Scottish titles, which have placed four in 10 roles at risk of redundancy.

The overhaul at Reach, which marks the latest in a string of job cuts in recent years, comes as its new chief executive tries to haul the struggling publisher into the digital era.

Reach’s heavy reliance on advertising has left it exposed, particularly as tech giants Facebook and Google roll out AI tools that are hitting web traffic across the industry.

The company has said it will establish a new network of reporters across the UK covering live and breaking news stories as it tries to cut down on duplication across its titles. It will also increase its video and audio output.

Piers North, who took over as chief executive this year, has also said the company will trial paywalls across its various titles in a bid to secure more revenues directly from readers.

Reach said it had been open and communicative about its use of AI.

A spokesman said: “Seismic changes in the industry demand decisive responses and a different way of working. 

“These changes also provide opportunities for us as we match our resources to our ambitions, significantly increasing our video output and setting up our live news network. This is why we have created 135 new roles to serve those opportunities.

“We will continue to engage closely with our unions as we work through the collective consultation process.”

data.title: Bari Weiss: The anti-woke superstar shaking up American journalism
data.description:
As America’s political and media elite partied in Washington, DC, following Donald Trump’s inauguration at the start of the year, one event in particular drew the great and the good.

The Free Press, an online news publication founded by outspoken US journalist Bari Weiss, teamed up with Uber and Elon Musk’s X for a party that played host to famous faces, including the Republican senator Ted Cruz, controversial Irish UFC star Conor McGregor and former prime minister Liz Truss.

The star-studded bash was a coup for Weiss, the former New York Times journalist who in recent years has become one of the hottest names in US media. Now, though, her star – and her fortune – have risen further after Hollywood giant Paramount inked a deal to buy The Free Press for a reported $150m (£111m) price tag.

The takeover embeds a news startup at the heart of one of America’s largest media empires. More controversially, however, the deal will also see Weiss take up the role of editor-in-chief of CBS News in a move that could herald the biggest overhaul in the Paramount-owned network’s near-100-year history.

Weiss, 41, rose to prominence during her time at the most staid outpost of US journalism – the New York Times.

On the newspaper’s opinion desk, Weiss gained notoriety for a string of barbed pieces bemoaning the rise of “woke” culture and what she viewed as the growing intolerance of the American liberal Left.

But it was in 2020, when Weiss resigned following a controversy over an opinion piece by US senator Tom Cotton calling for a military response to protests over the police killing of George Floyd, that the journalist took on an outsized position in the spotlight.

In a caustic 1,500-word open letter to the publisher of the New York Times, Weiss accused her colleagues of bullying and said the newspaper had fallen victim to “groupthink”. The New York Times said it was “committed to fostering an environment of honest, searching and empathetic dialogue between colleagues, one where mutual respect is required of all”.

After leaving the New York Times, Weiss launched her own Substack. From scrappy beginnings, The Free Press has quickly professionalised, with more than 50 employees and offices in New York and Los Angeles.

The publication now has 1.5 million subscribers, more than 170,000 of whom pay a monthly fee, putting it among the most-read – and lucrative – newsletters on Substack.

Weiss, who hosts her own podcast and was a guest at Jeff Bezos’s wedding earlier this year, has also demonstrated her nous as an entrepreneur, impressing industry executives at the exclusive Sun Valley Conference and attracting investment from the likes of Marc Andreessen and David Sacks.

For Weiss, independence has always been at the heart of The Free Press. Announcing the takeover to subscribers on Monday, she wrote: “From day one, the promise – and the business proposition – of this publication was simple: We would marry the quality of the old world to the freedom of the new. We would seek the truth and tell it plainly. And we would treat readers like adults capable of making their own choices.”

In some ways, the political leanings of The Free Press evade straightforward categorisation.

While the publication has often sided with the Trump administration – especially on cultural issues – it is by no means a pro-Trump outlet. After Disney’s ABC suspended late-night host Jimmy Kimmel last month, The Free Press ran an editorial accusing the US government of “jawboning” the broadcaster and warning of the threat to free speech.

Weiss, who has founded the free speech-focused University of Austin, favours the term “heterodox” to define her intellectual stance. In a clear indication of her non-partisan intentions, she wrote today that the US was facing the threat of illiberalism from both the “America-loathing far Left” and the “history-erasing far Right”.

Yet despite attempts to disown any ideological bent, there is a clear narrative strand running through Weiss’s output.

The Free Press is characterised by a disdain for policies or cultural trends deemed “woke”, reserving particular contempt for issues such as diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) programmes and cancel culture.

Weiss – who is Jewish and has written a book on anti-Semitism – has also put Israel at the centre of The Free Press’s output.

The publication has given extensive coverage to Columbia University – Weiss’s alma mater – over accusations it failed to protect Jewish students in the wake of the Oct 7 attacks. Columbia has since agreed to pay a $200m settlement to the Trump administration. Columbia said the settlement was not an admission of wrongdoing

In August, The Free Press published an article reporting that a number of malnourished Palestinian children whose images had appeared in major media outlets had pre-existing health conditions. While the reporting prompted clarifications from CNN and The Washington Post, it also attracted criticism owing to concerns that the output was attempting to downplay or even deny the famine conditions in Gaza.

The Free Press does publish stories on both sides of partisan divides. It has also attracted high-profile writers and its journalistic coups include the posthumous publication of letters penned by Russian dissident Alexei Navalny from his gulag prison cell.

Nevertheless, Weiss is viewed by many as an edgy new voice on the Right. Young, female and gay, she has carved out her place as an unorthodox modern mouthpiece for conservative values.

For her part, Weiss has made clear her ambitions to reshape CBS in the mould of The Free Press, describing it as a “once-in-a-lifetime opportunity”.

But by handing the top job at CBS News to a Right-leaning opinion writer with no broadcasting experience, Paramount risks the ire of both its audience and workforce.

The Free Press has previously taken aim at CBS in its own pages, publishing a scathing insider account of the network’s response to Oct 7. It has also been a vocal critic of NPR, the US public broadcaster that has been subjected to brutal funding cuts under Trump.

Insiders are reported to be both “apoplectic” and “literally freaking out” about Weiss’s arrival.

Aside from her editorial stance, Weiss has been branded a “wrecking ball” for her chaotic management style.

A number of high-profile journalists, including former senior New York Post editor Margi Conklin and Andy Mills, co-creator of the New York Times’s The Daily podcast, have left The Free Press in recent months. Weiss was approached for comment.

It comes at a febrile time for CBS owner Paramount, which was taken over by Skydance, the Hollywood studio run by David Ellison, in an $8bn deal this summer. Ellison, who is the son of Silicon Valley billionaire Larry and to whom Weiss will report directly, has insisted he will not politicise the network, but many remain unconvinced.

RedBird Capital, the US private equity firm seeking to lead a consortium to acquire The Telegraph, is owner of the second-largest shareholding in Paramount.

In the run-up to the takeover, Paramount settled a $16m lawsuit with Trump despite the claim being widely dismissed as baseless by legal experts. When Stephen Colbert, host of The Late Show, branded the settlement a “big fat bribe”, he was promptly taken off air.

Since the Skydance takeover, CBS has also overhauled editing rules on its Sunday morning Face the Nation show in a move that has also been viewed as a concession to the Trump administration. Paramount has declined to comment on these changes but denied that it struck any agreement with Trump ahead of the takeover.

Announcing his takeover of The Free Press on Monday, Ellison said it formed part of Paramount’s “bigger vision to modernise content”, adding: “We believe the majority of the country longs for news that is balanced and fact-based, and we want CBS to be their home.”

For Weiss, the deal cements the success of a newsletter she grew from nothing into a $150m business in just four years. There are no signs, however, that her ambitions will be satisfied.

“The values that we’ve hammered out here over the years – journalism based in curiosity and honesty, a culture of healthy disagreement, our shared belief in America’s promise – now have the opportunity to go very, very big,” she wrote.

data.title: Britain paying highest electricity prices in the world as net zero costs rise
data.description:
Ed Miliband’s net zero targets are facing fresh scrutiny after Britain was found to be paying the highest electricity prices in the developed world.

New data published on Tuesday showed the price paid by UK industry for power was 63pc higher than in France and 27pc higher than in Germany.

Britain is also the second-most expensive country in the world for household electricity, with billpayers paying twice as much as those in the US.

It came as Cornwall Insight also warned on Tuesday that household energy bills will jump by £100 in April.

The figures will increase pressure on the Energy Secretary, who has been accused of driving up electricity costs by adding green levies on to bills to fund renewables.

This includes billions of pounds in subsidies for carbon capture and wind farms, which have contributed to the UK paying the highest power prices in the world. Official figures on Tuesday had previously shown Britain had the highest prices of any country in 2023 as well, but on Tuesday that data was revised to show it was second place behind Slovakia. 

On Tuesday, Steve Elliott, chief executive of the Chemical Industries Association lobby group, warned: “We are competing for investment with these countries and we won’t get it if the Government continues to load policy costs on bills.”

But Mr Miliband has argued that switching to clean energy will pay off in the long run by cutting Britain’s reliance on volatile gas prices.

He has also repeatedly stressed that net zero was not to blame for soaring prices.

Ahead of his speech to the Labour Party conference on Tuesday, he told The Guardian: “Genuinely, I relish this fight on clean energy and net zero because that is what politics is about.

“The truth is, the reason why people’s living standards are stuck, why growth has been so low and public services are on their knees, is … trickle-down economics from the 80s, which left us with huge inequality and austerity in the 20s.”

However, his opponents dispute this and claim that Mr Miliband’s plan to switch Britain’s power system to 95pc clean energy by 2030 risks driving up prices even further.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said Mr Miliband’s plan “will lock us all into higher prices for longer” and lead to “higher bills for families and businesses are on the way”.

Richard Tice, Reform’s deputy leader, has also repeatedly lambasted “net stupid zero” and vowed to oppose “eco-zealot” Mr Miliband every step of the way.

He has vowed to use Reform-controlled councils to block planning applications for electricity infrastructure such as pylons.

The official figures published on Tuesday, which are produced by the International Energy Agency, showed that the average industrial electricity price in Britain hit 26.6p per kilowatt hour (kWh) in 2024, including taxes.

That was the highest figure of any developed country, compared to 16.3p in France, 21p in Germany and 23.5p in Italy.

Figures for the US were not published in the latest data, but figures for 2023 showed it was 6.5p per kWh.

Meanwhile, the picture was almost equally as bleak for households.

Britain was the second-highest for domestic electricity prices including tax, behind only Germany, at 30.45p per kWh.

In France, the equivalent price was 23.7p, while in the US it was 12.9p.

The data was released on the eve of the latest change in the price cap, which rose from £1720 to £1755 on October 1. Cornwall Insight has since forecast that this will fall to £1725 in January but jump by £100 in spring.

Groups for energy-intensive industries have warned that soaring power prices are forcing many British factories to close or shift production abroad.

The threat from net zero has also drawn scrutiny from Labour’s union backers, who are concerned that tens of thousands of industry jobs are disappearing with no immediate replacement. This problem is particularly acute in the North Sea.

On Monday, the GMB union warned that ceramics firms based in the historic Stoke-on-Trent potteries were being clobbered by high energy prices.

The union said that Labour risked losing support to Nigel Farage’s Reform Party if prices could not be brought down.

Sharon Yates, a ceramics worker, told the Labour Party conference: “If we do not help industries like ceramics with energy costs now – and invest in their cleaner future – we will keep losing decent, unionised jobs.

“When those jobs go, our communities go with them. People become desperate. And no one preys on desperate people more than Nigel Farage.”

A government spokesman said: “We are protecting energy-intensive businesses from volatile global fossil fuel markets, including slashing electricity costs by up to 25pc for manufacturing sectors and driving growth through our Plan for Change.

“Wholesale gas prices – which drive the overall cost of electricity – remain above their historic levels before Russia invaded Ukraine. That is the fossil fuel penalty being paid by businesses and our economy.

“That is why the only answer for Britain is our mission to get us off the rollercoaster of fossil fuel prices and onto clean, homegrown power we control, to bring down bills for good.”

data.title: Wasted wind power costs Britain £1bn
data.description:
Britain has spent more than £1bn on wasted wind power this year, raising questions over Ed Miliband’s plan to build thousands of new turbines across the UK.

The spiralling cost of switching off wind farms has come to light in new figures from tracker website Wasted Wind. It shows that the UK had spent £734m by this point in 2024.

This year’s bill has already topped £1bn because of growing strain on the electricity grid, which forces wind farms in remote areas to switch off when the network is too congested to transport their power elsewhere. This is known in the industry as curtailment.

At the same time, the imbalance requires energy officials to spend vast sums on replacement power, often from gas-fired plants.

The latest figures highlight the problems posed by Mr Miliband’s net zero push, as the Energy Secretary has vowed to accelerate the roll-out of renewables to help deliver green electricity across the UK by 2030.

So-called curtailment costs are ultimately passed on to customers via increases in their bills.

Regulator Ofgem recently warned that curtailment added around £15 to household bills as part of its latest price cap, which came into force on Wednesday.

A typical household will now pay up to £1,755 on gas and electricity a year, up from £1,720.

The increase has piled further pressure on Mr Miliband, who has vowed to cut household energy bills by £300 by the end of the decade.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, has branded the pledge a fantasy. She said: “By 2030, we will be paying £8bn to wind farms, not to generate any energy but simply to turn off when it’s too windy.

“The British people want us to prioritise cheap energy over wind developers’ profit and that’s what the Conservatives will do.”

Sharon Graham, general secretary of the Unite union, has branded Mr Miliband’s net zero plan a disaster and called on Sir Keir Starmer, the Prime Minister, to sack him.

Price pain

Her comments were made just hours after separate figures showed that Britain’s industry is paying the highest electricity prices in the developed world.

The UK is also the second-most expensive country for household electricity, with bills more than double those in the US.

Mr Miliband has repeatedly denied that net zero is to blame for higher bills and branded critics such as Nigel Farage, the leader of Reform UK, as “ideological extremists”.

Reform has vowed to scrap net zero if it wins the next election, while suggesting that it would look to restart fracking.

Speaking at the Labour Party conference, Mr Miliband said: “Fracking will not take a penny off bills, it will not create long-term sustainable jobs, it will trash our climate commitments, and it is dangerous and deeply harmful to our natural environment.

“The good news is communities have fought back and won that fight before and can do so again.”

He added: “We sent those frackers packing, I say let’s ban fracking and let’s vow to send this bunch of frackers packing too.”

Mr Miliband also insisted that clean energy offered the best chance to reduce Britain’s reliance on volatile gas prices.

Richard Tice, the deputy leader of Reform, said: “Labour has spent over £1bn paying to turn off wind turbines while relying on gas plants instead.

“This proves we remain heavily dependent on gas and that the Government’s fixation on green energy is costing taxpayers billions, driving up energy prices and pushing the country deeper into financial crisis.

“It’s time Ed Miliband dropped this reckless net zero agenda and backed a serious energy strategy: North Sea drilling, modular nuclear reactors and shale gas.

“To be a rich nation you need cheap energy. Net zero gives us the opposite.”

data.title: City banker faces two years in prison over ‘bogus’ sexual harassment claim
data.description:
A City banker is being sued for making a “wholly bogus” claim that he was sexually harassed by a female colleague.

Damilare Ajao, who worked for German finance giant Commerzbank in London, accused a female manager of commenting on his string vest under his shirt while they were in the staff canteen.

He also claimed he had to slap her hand away when she reached for his Gucci belt.

Mr Ajao was sacked in November 2019 but he subsequently brought the claim against the manager, identified only as “Q” in court, to an employment tribunal.

Tribunal judge Anthony Snelson threw out the claim, finding that the sexual harassment and assault allegations “were false and in large part made up”.

Commerzbank has now brought a High Court case against Mr Ajao accusing him of contempt of court for his false allegations.

If Mr Ajao is found in contempt of court, he could face a jail sentence of up to two years.

Banker ‘put forward lies as evidence’

Closing the bank’s case before Mr Justice Martin Spencer, barrister Louis Browne KC accused Mr Ajao of “rather wicked” behaviour in putting forward “lies” as evidence.

Mr Browne said: “What Mr Ajao has done and has continued to do is to manufacture a wholly bogus claim of sexual assault and harassment against ‘Q’, a claim he knew to be wholly bogus.”

The court heard Mr Ajao worked for the bank at its offices in Gresham Street between May and November 2019.

Among his unsuccessful complaints were that “Q” had a sexual motive in her dealings with him, and he claimed she became “annoyed” when he rejected her sexual advances.

Rejecting this accusation at the employment tribunal in April 2024, Judge Snelson said: “The acts and events on which he relies did not happen. There was no treatment of him by Q which could conceivably have been seen as amounting to harassment of any kind.”

Mr Ajao has now claimed that it was his “perception” he had been the victim of sexual harassment and assault, and that he was not deliberately untruthful.

Sexual harassment was ‘perceived’, banker claims

But Mr Browne said that at the employment tribunal, Mr Ajao was “very clear... not that there was a perception that he was sexually harassed or assaulted”.

He said the manager named as “Q” had gone on to “develop a serious psychiatric illness” as a result of the disproved allegations.

Mr Ajao’s barrister Sasha Wass KC said there was “foundation in evidence” for everything he said, adding the manager accepted there had been a conversation – although innocent – about his string vest.

She said it should be taken to be a “realistic possibility” that Mr Ajao honestly perceived the comment as sexually harassing.

She added: “Had the evidence been that Q had never ever commented on his undergarments, then that would be a very different position, because there would be no room for misunderstanding.”

The hearing continues.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Thames Water lenders prepared to take £7bn hit to avoid nationalisation
data.description:
A group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to effectively write off £7.5bn of debt in a bid to avoid nationalisation.

The consortium has proposed a turnaround plan that would cancel around a third of Thames Water’s near-£20bn debt pile and commit the group to investing in the company.

Outstanding fines would be paid – but the investor group, dubbed London & Valley Water, would also seek leniency on future penalties in exchange for investment.

Thames Water revealed over the summer that annual losses had hit £1.6bn as a soaring debt burden and continued sewage failures fuelled its financial crisis.

Bosses said it would take at least a decade to turn around Britain’s largest water company.

The company’s creditors are looking to secure backing for their plans to avoid the utility being put into a temporary special administration regime, which would effectively wipe out their investments.

Lenders said they would not pay a dividend while conducting their turnaround plans or until the company is listed.

They also committed not to sell the business before the end of March 2030.

The consortium said the “more ambitious” plan would increase investment in Thames Water by around £1bn compared to proposals they submitted to Ofwat in May.

The latest proposal was the result of “three months of detailed discussion” with the water regulator and would “support long-term financial resilience and is intended to help the company pay for outstanding fines”, it added.

Mike McTighe, a City veteran whose current roles include chairman of Telegraph Media Group, would become chairman of Thames Water under the creditors’ proposals.

He said: “There is a huge amount of work to be done to turn around Thames Water and deliver the improved service and environmental outcomes that customers and local communities deserve.

“From day one, we will inject billions in new investment, strengthen Thames Water’s balance sheet, transform the company for thousands of hard-working frontline staff and begin the delivery of an operational turnaround that puts 16 million customers and the environment first.”

Lenders of Thames Water’s “Class A” debt would receive at least 10pc of new equity in the utility company in exchange for writing off about 25pc of their stake, worth around £4bn.

Thames Water said it remains in discussions over the bid but any agreement would be subject to High Court approval.

Chris Weston, the chief executive of Thames Water, said: “Today is an important milestone in our ongoing work with creditors and stakeholders to secure a market-led recapitalisation that establishes the financial and regulatory foundations required to support the investment and performance improvements our customers expect.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain needs a million extra builders to hit Labour’s targets
data.description:
Britain needs one million extra builders to deliver Labour’s construction and housing targets, the Chartered Institute of Building (CIOB) has warned.

Large numbers of new tradesmen, ranging from bricklayers to electricians, are needed to reach the Government’s aim of building 1.5 million homes by 2029, as well as to meet net zero property targets, decarbonise the country’s energy system and deliver other major infrastructure projects.

The CIOB said in its report that the scale and speed of today’s building boom could exceed those of post-war Britain or the transition from an industrial to a services-based economy in the 1980s.

It said: “Already in the pipeline there is Labour’s ambitious 1.5 million house building target and plans to decarbonise the building stock and energy system. On top of this there is a backlog of much-needed construction work to be delivered.

“Construction output may need to expand by up to 40pc to meet the Government’s stated ambitions. This, potentially, could mean construction needing to attract more than one million new recruits.

“And, in the absence of investing heavily in UK construction products manufacturing, it would mean spending billions of pounds more on imported materials.”

The CIOB’s estimates suggest a radical expansion of the construction sector is needed. Official figures show there are around 2.1 million workers currently employed in the industry.

Reliance on overseas workers

The scale of expansion required will raise fresh questions about how feasible Labour’s goals are, particularly given the construction sector has been shrinking.

A wave of retirement has led to a decline of around 150,000 workers over the past five years.

The industry has become increasingly reliant on overseas workers to address labour and skills shortages. Census figures show the percentage of builders in England and Wales born outside the UK rose from 10.6pc to 16.7pc in the decade to 2021.

The Government is under pressure to tighten immigration rules as it seeks to curb a recent surge in immigration. It suggests building companies may struggle to rely solely on overseas labour in future.

Brian Green, author of the CIOB report, said companies would have to encourage people in other sectors to move into construction and pay them more in order to meet the Government’s stretching goals.

As well as its much-publicised housebuilding plan, ministers have set a target of replacing 600,000 gas boilers with heat pumps each year.

Ministers also want all privately rented homes to have an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) of C or above by 2030, which will require upgrades to more than a million homes.

Ed Miliband’s goal to decarbonise Britain’s energy system by the end of the decade will meanwhile involve the biggest programme of pylon construction in a generation, as well as other corresponding infrastructure such as substations.

Despite calls for a rapid increase in recruitment, the latest S&P Global UK construction purchasing managers’ index (PMI) found that jobs in the sector declined for the ninth month in a row in September, marking the longest period of contraction since the pandemic. Activity also declined for the ninth month in a row, with residential, commercial and civil engineering work all struggling.

Tim Moore, at S&P Global, said companies “reported caution among clients ahead of the autumn Budget and a general reluctance to commit to major capital expenditure projects against a subdued domestic economic backdrop”.

A government spokesman said: “Our planning reforms are driving UK housebuilding to its highest level in over 40 years to build the 1.5 million homes we need.

“We are backing the Construction Skills Mission Board’s initiative to recruit 100,000 extra construction workers per year by 2029 and investing £1.2bn in skills, alongside creating 32 new Homebuilding Skills Hubs – training bricklayers, joiners and electricians we need to get Britain building.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Channel 4 and BBC strike streaming deal in Netflix fightback
data.description:
Classic BBC shows are set to appear on Channel 4’s streaming platform following a landmark deal between the two broadcasters.

Hundreds of titles from UKTV, which is owned by the BBC’s commercial arm, will be available on Channel 4’s streaming service from next year, including hits such as The Office, Red Dwarf and QI.

UKTV’s output also includes original series such as Outrageous, a historical drama about the Mitford sisters and the Jersey-based crime drama Bergerac.

The tie-up comes as the UK’s public service broadcasters attempt to step up their collaboration to counter the influence of deep-pocketed US streaming giants, including Netflix and Amazon Prime.

Speaking at the RTS Cambridge conference last month, the chief executives of the BBC, ITV, Channel 4 and Channel 5 all called for an easing of regulations to allow broadcasters to form “strategic partnerships” that would enable them to better compete.

Competition rules prevent broadcasting from merging over fears that such deals would grant them too much sway over TV advertising. However, UKTV bosses argue that the rules are redundant in the age of streaming and YouTube.

Similar broadcaster collaborations

Broadcasters have been increasingly finding ways to collaborate more closely without explicitly violating competition rules. Over the summer, Channel 4, ITV and Sky agreed to jointly sell their advertising space while keeping the revenues from sales discreet.

ITV has also struck a deal with Disney, which will see the two companies share programmes including Love Island and The Bear on each other’s streaming platforms.

However, British TV bosses have pushed back against calls for more fundamental consolidation, insisting that full-blown mergers would not be beneficial to the industry.

The UKTV agreement marks the first time Channel 4 has carried a third-party service on its streaming platform. The deal will boost Channel 4’s business by giving it access to a broader range of programming against which to sell advertising.

UKTV will, in turn, be able to reach Channel 4’s traditionally younger audience. This is considered key as the BBC and other broadcasters attempt to stem an exodus of Gen Z viewers to YouTube.

UKTV – the home to channels such as Dave, Drama and Yesterday – will continue to operate its standalone streaming platform U.

Jonathan Allan, interim chief executive of Channel 4, told The Telegraph: “This ground-breaking collaboration combines UKTV’s programming library with Channel 4’s powerful reach among younger audiences.

“It not only enhances Channel 4’s streaming offer to viewers and advertisers, but also introduces a new generation to beloved British content.”

Marcus Arthur, chief executive of UKTV, said: “Following record viewing of our U streaming service last year, I’m delighted to be expanding our relationship with Channel 4 through this carriage deal and offering viewers even more opportunities to watch U’s rich mix of content.

“UKTV has a successful history of building scale through partnerships, and this exciting opportunity demonstrates the power of media companies collaborating to drive sustained growth, with clear benefits for UKTV, Channel 4 and above all, viewers.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: No washing line and £100k church repair bills: beware the archaic property covenant
data.description:
Antonella Lazzeri, 59, lives in a street of 1930s houses in Bournemouth where there is a covenant forbidding owners from hanging washing in their gardens, although none of them adhere to it.

“I can only assume it was due to snobbery,” the journalist says. Maybe drying washing on a line was seen as common in those days and it may have been the local council trying to ensure standards didn’t slip.

“I’m not sure where you were supposed to dry the laundry – especially in those days. It made me laugh when I first read it. I asked a lawyer about covenants but he said a lot of them were unenforceable.”

Restrictive covenants, sometimes described as permanent legal handcuffs on a property, are not uncommon but can come as a nasty surprise to buyers or new owners who aren’t expecting them.

The legal stipulations run with the land, meaning they apply to all future owners.

Some are clearly beneficial to owners, including shielding properties and neighbours from disturbances. But others hark back to a different way of life and no longer make sense in the modern world.

As a result, owners can find their homes saddled with near-meaningless restrictions such as not being able to keep pigeons, tan leather or have a slaughterhouse – even though the garden today may only be 15 sq ft.

Covenants such as this originate from contractual agreements between two landowners where one party agrees to limit what they do on their property for the benefit of the other.

They are written into a property’s title deeds or lease agreement and as such any new covenants must be registered with the Land Registry.

When Victoria Clark, 50, bought her early Georgian Grade II-listed farmhouse last year, lurking in the small print of the title deeds was a note banning any “feathers and hooves” from the granary behind the house.

“It was a working farm until 2005, when the farmer moved into a converted barn and sold the main farmhouse. At this point, the covenant was established – so it’s not historical at all,” the business owner explains. “It was about the farmer protecting his privacy.”

While the feathers-and-hooves covenant wasn’t a concern, there were a few clauses that they did want to change before purchasing, including one that stipulated the house and granary couldn’t be joined together. Addressing this represented a deal-breaker for the couple.

“Our neighbour was very amenable and, together with the vendor, we agreed to rewrite some of the clauses between us, sent them to one solicitor and the others were instructed to approve it,” explains Clark.

“In a sense, we were future-proofing as much as anything when it comes to selling the property.”

Others are less easy to shake off. For owners of much-desired old vicarages the historic rules can result in devastating charges.

In 2003, a couple living in the Warwickshire village of Aston Cantlow were told they were responsible for chancel repair costs to the tune of £100,000. Gail and Andrew Wallbank inherited Glebe Farm and with it the liability for repairs to the nearby St John the Baptist church, dating from when the church gave the farm 2.75 acres of land 800 years previously.

Chancel repair liability is a medieval covenant, which means that some houses located next to churches, including vicarages and rectories, can be asked to contribute towards the maintenance work of the church. It dates back to the time when church repairs were the responsibility of the parish rector and paid for from tithes or local taxes.

They took the case to the House of Lords but lost; resulting in a court order of around £230,000 to repair the church and faced a legal bill of approximately £250,000. In 2009, the Wallbanks sold the house at auction to help cover the payment.

A change in the law meant that churches had until October 2013 to protect their right to chancel repair liability by registering a notice at the Land Registry against affected properties. More than 12,000 properties had notices registered against them.

The problem is that around 13pc of land remains unregistered – leaving prospective buyers in the dark about their purchase. However, the Law Commission is consulting on reforms to ensure that chancel repair liability doesn’t bind buyers of land, unless it is registered – and therefore visible – to purchasers.

If owners find themselves tied by the same covenant, the risk can be offset by buying a chancel repair liability insurance for the period of ownership.

It’s not just old properties that fall into this realm. Developers usually include property covenants on new housing developments as a way of maintaining a desired standard and uniformity, thereby protecting property values and preventing nuisances for owners.

Anyone owning a house in the King’s new town of Poundbury in Dorset must apply to the Duchy of Cornwall for written approval to alter or extend the exterior of their property. Those wanting to park a caravan “or other similar chattel” in their parking space similarly need to first apply for consent of the Prince of Wales, who manages the Duchy.

A spokesman for the Duchy of Cornwall said the guidelines for external appearance of properties ensured the community maintained its local distinctiveness and character to which all residents had committed.

Making a local contribution

In some cases, it can be helpful to take a pragmatic view of property covenants, says Charlie Wells, of buying agency Prime Purchase.

“I was buying a manor farmhouse for a client in the Cotswolds. For 30 years, the vendors had allowed a dog show to take place on their land. The vendors felt very strongly about the village and said they would look more favourably on a buyer who would accept a covenant to allow the show to continue.”

While he accepts most buyers would have pushed back against such a clause, these clients felt it was a nice thing to do.

“Their lawyers wanted to make any clause that was registered against the title unbinding, but my clients were happy to have it in place for the duration of their occupation of the property and it was written into the contract,” explains Wells.

“It’s not rare for events such as cricket matches, dog shows or village fetes to have historically taken place on a significant property. Quite often, buyers commit to these at their discretion and such events continue. It is always important that the buyer takes legal advice and doesn’t accept such covenants and easements without consultation.”

However, for buyers looking from abroad, the revelation of an archaic rule can be a confusing deterrent, says Sarah Conway, of law firm Maurice Turnor Gardner.

“The whole market has changed since Covid and people want certainty. We have such a historic, near-feudal system in this country and trying to explain it to an American or Saudi buyer is sometimes challenging.”

Conway cites an example from several years ago when a wealthy Eastern European client was in the process of buying a large country estate and couldn’t understand why members of the public were permitted to use the footpaths that crossed his land.

The rights to which a property is subject and owners must also adhere, are often just as onerous as restrictive covenants; particularly in the case of large country estates. As a rule, public footpaths and bridle paths must be maintained and well signposted by the landowner. Attempts to divert these are met with a bureaucratic process with high hurdles to clear, including public support.

“He was used to being able to stand on the Eurasian Steppe and use his hunting rifle whenever he pleased and was so frustrated that this wouldn’t be possible in this new property that he backed out of the sale altogether.”

City properties are restricted too

London houses aren’t immune to property covenants either; some have historic restrictions that prevent the manufacture of bricks or the sale of intoxicating liquor.

“We have also seen specific covenants imposed where part of a piece of land is sold off and, for example, a terrace or balcony cannot be used to sit on,” says Conway.

Many agents agree that restrictions imposed by covenants can affect the future resale potential of a property by deterring buyers who are looking for freedom to make alterations.

There are properties with very specific restrictive covenants imposed due to their proximity to sensitive governmental sites.

“In some cases, with properties in key central locations, covenants allow for members of armed security forces to access a property when high-profile events are taking place,” says Conway. “Some owners don’t mind; they find it reassuring.”

In theory, most restrictive covenants are enforceable without limitation of time, albeit many are very old and enforcement is unlikely. “Banks these days are very cautious, however, so if there is a covenant on the property, they’ll likely insist the owner takes out indemnity insurance,” warns Conway.

A buyer and seller can agree to try and remove a covenant but it requires the consent of the person who benefits from the covenant. Assuming that person is not the seller, that person might require compensation. If the beneficiary doesn’t agree or if it’s not possible to locate them, you have to make an application to the Upper Tribunal for the covenant to be released.

This process can be costly and time-consuming, points out Angela Kerr, of HomeOwners Alliance, a property advice organisation. And as with all litigation, there is no guarantee of success.

data.title: ‘Expedia’s mistake cost me my dream adventure to Kenya’
data.description:
Has a company treated you unfairly? Our Consumer Champion is available to help. For how to contact her, click here.*

Dear Katie,

I was due to fly to Kenya in July on a trip that I’d been saving up for all year, since I’m a university student with limited funds.

The flights cost £637 which came out of my bank account. Subsequently, I was invited to a wedding which clashed with the dates, so I rang Expedia, which I booked through, to change my outbound flight date. The customer service agent found me a replacement outbound flight from Gatwick.

I was asked to pay a $90 (£67) fee to change the flight, so I provided my credit card details over the phone. I received two confirmation emails from Expedia stating that the payment had been received and the changes had been made. I also received emails from Uganda Airlines confirming the new flight dates and times.

To my extreme disappointment when I reached the check-in desk at Gatwick I was denied boarding on the flight. I had arrived early at about 4.30pm and the man behind the Uganda Airlines desk told me that there was a problem with my ticket. He told me “there is no ticket attached to your reservation”.

I was asked to wait while the matter was resolved. About 15 minutes later, I was called over by a lady on the Gatwick check-in team who had been liaising with Uganda Airlines. I was told that there had been a payment issue: the $90 payment to change flights had not been taken.

I expected that if I paid what I owed everything would be fine. However, I was told that I would need to pay the full cost of a ticket in order to check in, which was about £790. I declined this offer as I could not afford it.

I then called Expedia trying to find a way to check in and board the flight, but I was told that nothing could be done because Uganda Airlines customer service desks were shut for the weekend.

The Expedia lady on the phone sent me an email, with my ticket numbers, for one final try at the check-in desk – but the Gatwick check-in staff insisted there was nothing they could do.

Over the next three weeks, I called Expedia several times and also sent it several emails. It always promised to get back in touch within three days but it never did.

It once emailed me to ask for extra information, including a puzzling statement that my flight booking was with British International Helicopters, a company I’ve never even heard of. It says it has tried to claim a refund from Uganda Airlines, however there has been no progress for three weeks.

Instead of going to Kenya, I have been on a busking tour of the UK with my saxophone to try to raise more money for another trip. I would appreciate your help to recover the money.

– DE, via email

Dear DE,

I was extremely sorry to hear that this trip, for which you had been saving up all year, had been ruined over a payment issue you knew nothing about.

When I asked Expedia, it said its records showed your flight with Uganda Airlines was confirmed, meaning you should have been able to board. It said it was still awaiting further details from the airline to clarify what happened at boarding.

Then some days later, it wrote “sincerely apologising” for the delay in resolving this matter for you and accepted responsibility for you being denied boarding.

A spokesman said: “In situations like this, we often need to coordinate closely with our airline partners and, unfortunately, that process took longer than expected.”

You have now received a full refund for the £657 flight booking, plus £100 in compensation, which brings this matter to a close.

Next time you book flights, I might recommend booking directly with the airline as it may be cheaper and you won’t face layers of extra admin where internal errors such as this one can occur.

data.title: The London Stock Exchange is on a long and sad path to oblivion
data.description:
There were some rare pieces of good news for London’s moribund new issues market last week.

First, the Chancellor, Rachel Reeves, was reported to be considering exempting stamp duty for newly-listed company shares in next month’s Budget. If that were to happen, it would indeed be welcome.

Second, the drought in new issues was broken by Beauty Tech Group, a Cheshire-based enterprise that makes at-home cosmetic devices used by the likes of Kim Kardashian, with a stock market debut that valued the company at more than £300m.

This was also welcome, the more so as the company’s founder and chief executive, Lawrence Newman, unashamedly cited patriotic reasons for floating in the UK rather than elsewhere. We need more entrepreneurs like him.

Sadly, that’s where the good news ends, for it says something about the supposed attractions of London as a place for share listings that this was the biggest new issue so far this year.

You’ve got to start somewhere, and a £300m valuation is certainly not to be sneezed at, but compared to what’s happening elsewhere in the world, this is a tiddler which is going to make little or no difference to the underlying trend.

Like the UK economy as a whole, this trend represents a steady relative decline. Data collated by Bloomberg shows that so far this year, the London Stock Exchange (LSE) has slipped out of the top 20 markets for new listings for the first time ever.

Even Mexico and Qatar are now doing better than the LSE. Other comparative measures of the LSE’s performance appear even more stark. In terms of the amount raised from new listings, the LSE has plunged nearly 70pc to the smallest haul in 35 years.

It would be nice to think that with the right regulatory and tax environment, this dire state of affairs could be reversed.

Hope springs eternal, and many still dream of a “wider still and wider” return to the glory days of old. Alas, it’s largely delusional.

You have to go all the way back to the pre-First World War age, when London was at the centre of a vast empire covering a quarter of the world’s population, to see the LSE at the height of its powers.

Like some colossus, it bestrode the world, seemingly unstoppable in its power and reach.

However, times have changed, and with the empire now gone, the LSE struggles to maintain its position in a world that is increasingly competitive for international capital and hostile to the UK.

The comedown is proving a difficult one. Britain still has a long way to go in coming to terms with this wrenching change in its circumstances, now a mere bit player at best, rather than the powerhouse it once was.

Share trading is, of course, a relatively small part of today’s London Stock Exchange Group; years of diversification have made the company into a much more broadly based provider of financial data and infrastructure.

The stock market is also a virtual irrelevance to the wider City, which encompasses a multitude of financial service industries, ranging from insurance to banking and from foreign exchange to derivatives and clearing.

Even so, the exchange’s fortunes still count for something, and as powerfully symbolic of Britain’s diminishing status in the world, they do not make for comfortable viewing.

Once the natural home for Britain’s available pool of capital, and that of its empire, the LSE is these days hemmed in by competition. The options are almost limitless, and sadly, the choice when exercised tends not to be the LSE. Capital is, instead, increasingly flowing into overseas markets and private equity.

Reversing this trend is not simply a matter of tweaking the regulations or, indeed, the tax treatment of equity trading.

Britain’s cultural aversion to high levels of executive pay and the continued obsession with non-commercial, environmental, social and governance (ESG) issues plainly don’t help.

It would, for instance, be hard to imagine UK investors tolerating the $1tn (£740bn) in performance-related pay that the Tesla board has just approved for Elon Musk in the US. The ESG brigade would scream blue murder.

Musk may be an extreme example, but the truth is that executive pay in the FTSE 100 lags significantly behind what is available elsewhere in the world, as well as in private equity.

Also true is that the UK’s listing rules remain demanding by international standards, even after attempts to loosen them by removing the distinction between premium and standard listings.

But as I say, this is not the nub of the problem. Rather, it is that capital is increasingly bypassing the LSE to chase the supposedly higher returns of private equity and the greater liquidity – and therefore enhanced valuations – of Wall Street and Asia.

These are far from healthy trends. The great advantage of the publicly listed UK corporation is that it ensures a degree of transparency and accountability that doesn’t exist in private equity and is generally deficient in the passive investment strategies that drive investment in overseas markets.

Rewind thirty years to the 1990s, and Britain’s big occupational pension funds were still the mainstay of the London stock market.

This, in turn, was underpinned by the so-called “cult of equity”, a way of thinking about equity investment pioneered in the UK by George Ross Goobey, head of the Imperial Tobacco pension fund. Believing, rightly, that equities always outperform the alternatives over time, pension fund trustees poured their money into the London stock market.

However, the abolition of the tax credit on dividends and other catastrophic public policy errors soon followed.

The big pension funds closed their doors to new members and began capping their liabilities by rotating out of UK equities into government bonds and other supposedly risk-free assets.

Globalisation was the final coup de grâce; it was hard to justify being overweight in UK equities when they accounted for an ever-diminishing share of the global total.

Other countries have been more successful at ensuring that domestic savings flow into homegrown equities, but it’s a hard sell when the returns elsewhere look so much higher.

It’s also a vicious circle. Declining UK stock market liquidity makes it more likely that successful UK companies list overseas, where the valuations are higher. Squeezed liquidity thereby begets still scarcer liquidity.

No doubt there will be occasional reprieves; once the artificial intelligence bubble goes pop, as inevitably it will, the UK stock might seem relatively attractive once more, if only because it has hardly any exposure to AI.

But they will be temporary; the overriding trajectory is one of decline, and it’s hard to see what can be done to reverse it.

A useful starting place might nonetheless be a more business-friendly environment, with appropriately pro-growth regulation and taxation to match.

For now, it seems like a lost cause. But get these things right, and more companies like Beauty Tech would soon be beating a path to the LSE’s door.
