data.title: Santander hires former head of British Army
data.description:
Santander has hired the former head of the British Army to boost defence lending amid a spending boom since the start of the Ukraine war.

General Sir Patrick Sanders, who was chief of the general staff in the British Army from 2022 to 2024, will join Santander to help steer its approach to working with the defence industry.

The former army chief will advise the bank on lending to arms companies at a time when European governments are looking to pour hundreds of billions into the defence industry to help protect against the threat from Vladimir Putin.

Nato’s European members last year delivered a double-digit boost to military spending, hitting $454bn (£337bn). However, some estimates suggest that to reach the alliance’s new 2035 target of spending 5pc of GDP on defence, they will need to allocate more than £500bn a year on top of this.

Darren Jones, head of global banking at Santander, said: “We are seeing growing demand for financial products and services from companies across the defence supply chain, particularly within the EU.

“Supporting this sector is critical for the security of the region, and as one of Europe’s largest banks, we recognise the important role we must play.

“The appointment of General Sanders as a senior adviser to our business underscores both the strategic importance of the industry and our commitment to supporting its continued development.”

The appointment comes amid a period of upheaval at the Spanish bank’s British operations.

Mike Regnier, the UK chief executive of Santander, last week announced his departure to pursue “other interests,” saying someone else should oversee Santander’s integration of TSB, the high-street rival it bought this summer for £2.6bn.

Mr Regnier had been overseeing a plan to shutter about one-fifth of the bank’s UK branches.

In January, Santander’s UK chairman, William Vereker, quit amid reports of a rift with the company’s executive chairwoman, Ana Botin. His departure came weeks after reports suggested Santander was considering leaving Britain over excessive red tape.

Ms Botin later insisted the bank will remain in the UK “into the future”, even as she warned that Europe was in danger of becoming a “museum” filled with over-regulated firms.

Turning point for banks

General Sanders’s appointment marks a turning point after banks have faced criticism for environmental, social and governance-focused rules that, in some cases, prevent them from investing in defence companies.

However, the growing threat of war and subsequent rise in defence spending have seen investors reassess their position.

Shares in weapons makers, including BAE Systems, Thales and Rheinmetall, have surged since the start of the Ukraine war as European governments have increased spending on defence.

Sir Keir Starmer has pledged to increase UK defence spending to 2.5pc of the country’s GDP by April 2027, with an ambition to hit 3pc during the next Parliament, while the EU has made similar pledges.

Boris Pistorius, German defence minister, said at the weekend that the massive investment into the defence industry had created a kind of “gold rush fever”.

General Sanders has warned the UK should ready itself for war with Russia including by replenishing its weapons stockpiles and bolstering its air defences to prepare.

The 59-year-old served 38 years in the British Army, where he commanded operations in countries including Northern Ireland, Kosovo, Bosnia, Iraq and Afghanistan.

Spanish bankers and policymakers have been debating how to boost bank lending to the defence sector. Ms Botin has said banks should be allowed to lend to defence projects using the “buffer” reserves of capital that regulators require them to hold against potential future losses.

Santander’s own policies block it from investing in certain types of arms, including landmines and ammunition containing depleted uranium, which have both been used in Ukraine.

The bank is also required to consider “whether goods may cause or exacerbate armed conflicts or aggravate existing tensions in the final destination country”.

data.title: AstraZeneca stokes fears of London exit with New York listing
data.description:
AstraZeneca has announced plans to list shares on the New York Stock Exchange, stoking fears that Britain’s biggest drugmaker could quit London.

In a shake-up announced on Monday, the FTSE pharmaceuticals giant said it would upgrade its US listing to ensure it is equally as important in New York as it is on the London Stock Exchange.

Bosses said the move would make it easier for the company to raise money from US investors, describing it as “a global listing for global investors in a global company”.

As part of the announcement, the company said it would maintain its London listing and remain headquartered in the UK.

However, this move will stoke fears over AstraZeneca’s future in London. Analysts at AJ Bell said it hinted “at the possibility of a more dramatic shift at some point in the future”.

Mark Kelly, the chief executive of corporate advisory firm MKP Advisors, said he expected AstraZeneca would leave the London market within the next two or three years, adding: “This is a halfway house, it’s potentially a backdoor route to an eventual full US listing.

“I don’t think anyone is necessarily surprised to see AstraZeneca going this way.”

Ketan Patel, a fund manager at AstraZeneca shareholder Whitefriars, said the risk of the company quitting London was a “concern for the UK market”. He said its exit would come as a “huge blow” to UK investors.

Such concerns will no doubt sound alarm bells for the Labour Government, which is currently trying to rally support among businesses at its party conference in Liverpool.

AstraZeneca, which was the UK’s most valuable listed company up until last week, has long been viewed as one of the businesses most likely to ditch its London listing.

Earlier this year, reports suggested that Sir Pascal Soriot, AstraZeneca’s chief executive, was keen to move the company’s main listing to New York – owing to growing frustration with the UK.

Sir Pascal described the drugmaker as a “very American company”.

The business was formed in 1999 through the merger of Sweden’s Astra AB and Britain’s Zeneca PLC. It remains headquartered in Cambridge.

Mr Kelly said: “AstraZeneca is only really here because when they did the original AstraZeneca merger, it made most sense at the time – London was a bastion of financial equity markets, and it was a wonderful place for a massive company to be listed. It’s difficult to make that argument in 2025.”

Mr Patel said: “There is a clear strategy towards a business that is US-focused, and I would not be surprised if a full exit from London is on the cards. The company already reports and pays dividends in US dollars.”

Currently, more than 40pc of AstraZeneca’s revenue comes from the US, making it the company’s largest market by a significant margin. China accounts for 13pc of revenues, while Europe stands at 21pc.

In recent months, AstraZeneca has been stepping up investment in the US – including a $50bn (£37bn) announcement – following demands from Donald Trump, the US president, for companies to invest more there.

At the same time, the pharmaceutical company has pulled a series of investments from Britain amid an ongoing row with ministers over high medicine charges.

Earlier this month, AstraZeneca halted a £200m investment in its Cambridge research facility after the Government walked away from talks to lower drug charges for medicines in the NHS.

This has fuelled claims that Britain is becoming “uninvestable” for drugmakers.

The Cambridge move followed an earlier decision by AstraZeneca to walk away from a £450m investment in a Liverpool vaccine factory in protest over the Government reducing funding for the site.

Sean Conroy, an analyst at Shore Capital, said the latest move from AstraZeneca could arguably be “a lever and a negotiation tactic to be able to demonstrate that it is cosying up to the US a bit more” – putting Britain under pressure at the same time.

data.title: Banks ordered to snoop on pensioners
data.description:
Banks will be required to monitor pensioners’ bank accounts and share payments data with the Government under a new fraud bill, The Telegraph understands.

The accounts of all those receiving three benefits including universal credit, pension credit and employment support Allowance will be monitored for fraud and error under measures being proposed to claw back £9.6bn by 2030.

The new powers will be granted under the Public Authorities (Fraud, Error and Recovery) Bill which is due back in the House of Lords this month.

The legislation will require banks to share information with the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) to identify incorrect benefits payments.

An estimated £9.5bn was lost last year due to benefit overpayments and fraudulent applications, the equivalent of 3.3pc of the total benefit bill.

While the scheme will be aimed at those on universal credit and other employment benefits, it would also include retirees in receipt of pension credit. An estimated £610m in pension credit was overpaid because of DWP errors last year, £270m of which was as a result of fraud.

Banks forbidden from telling customers

Financial institutions will have to share data with the DWP and will be forbidden from telling their customers that they have done so. In cases of both fraud and error, ministers will be able to demand that the money is repaid directly from a “relevant account”.

The Government insisted that it would not have access to bank accounts and that decisions about benefit entitlement would be taken by a human.

As many as 1.36 million pensioners were on pension credit in February 2025. Applications for the benefit soared to record levels after Labour introduced winter fuel reforms last year, with 117,800 approved by February.

It comes just weeks after HMRC restarted controversial “direct recovery” powers which allow it to take underpaid tax directly from bank accounts, where what is owed is more than £1,000.

Banks have raised serious concerns about the powers granted to the DWP in the bill, as well as the challenges of combing through the account data of benefit claimants.

Under current rules, banks have to report suspicious activity to the National Crime Agency in cases of suspected money laundering. Lenders are not responsible for monitoring benefit fraud or overpayments.

UK Finance, which represents banks and building societies, said the move would be “operationally challenging” for lenders and warned it could “cause unintended harm to vulnerable individuals”.

‘Unintended harm’

Peter Tyler, of the trade body, said: “We have concerns about certain provisions in the Public Authorities (Fraud, Error and Recovery) Bill – particularly the proposed power for DWP to recover funds directly from customers’ accounts.

“It raises the risk of causing unintended harm to vulnerable individuals and is likely to be operationally challenging.”

A spokesman for campaign group Big Brother Watch said that the incoming legislation was unprecedented as it gives intervention powers for government error and not just if there was a suspicion of benefit fraud.

The spokesman said: “It’s unprecedented because we’ve not really seen this type of generalised financial surveillance on a population-wide scale where there’s actually no suspicion of fraud.”

‘Rushing through legislation’

Helen Whately, the shadow work and pensions secretary, said: “It’s clear that Labour ministers are rushing through this legislation to grant themselves sweeping powers without proper checks and balances.

“If Labour were serious about tackling real fraud and controlling the welfare bill, they would back our proposals to deal with so-called ‘sickfluencers’ and ensure that claimants are attending face-to-face assessments.”

John O’Connell, of the TaxPayers’ Alliance campaign group, said: “Taxpayers young and old will be deeply concerned about some of the proposals outlined in this bill.

“Even with strict safeguards, introducing new powers to retrieve cash directly from bank accounts is a seriously concerning step.”

The use of AI by the banks is not new. The technology is used to screen payments for fraud and to give customer warnings, as well as to clean up data so it can be more easily shared or used.

A DWP spokesman said: “In cases of fraud and error, a human will always make any decisions that affect benefit entitlement.

“All powers in the Fraud, Error and Recovery Bill are underpinned by a principle of fairness and proportionality, with numerous safeguards and independent oversight in place.

“We have a duty to the taxpayer and this Bill is set to save £1.5bn over the next five years, which together with wider reforms will save £9.6bn by 2030, according to OBR estimates.”

data.title: BBC apologises for calling October 7 attacks an ‘escalation’
data.description:
The BBC has apologised for referring to the October 7 massacre as “escalations”.

The wording was used in an email to BBC News staff marking two years since the attacks.

Rather than use a picture of Israeli victims of the Hamas atrocity, an accompanying image showed Palestinian women and children picking their way through rubble in Gaza.

The email was part of a regular series of updates sent by the BBC Audiences team, which analyses audience data and advises journalists and programme-makers on how best to tailor content to viewers and listeners.

It said: “As we reach the two-year anniversary of the escalations in the Israel-Gaza conflict, we asked UK audiences what they want and need from news coverage moving forward.”

The wording of the email has fuelled fresh allegations of anti-Israel bias within the BBC, which has faced repeated such accusations since October 7.

Adam Ma’anit, whose cousin, Tsachi Idan, was taken hostage from the Nihal Oz kibbutz on October 7 and murdered in captivity, condemned the BBC.

He said: “To use that language and framing is incendiary and insulting.

“That this report was circulated today of all days speaks to an institutional bias within the BBC that is shocking and distressing. Does our pain not count?”

Natalie Sanandaji, a survivor of the October 7 Nova massacre, said: “To make such a ‘mistake’ on October 7 of all days is painful for me to see as a survivor of the Nova festival. Once again, it feels the media is trying to downplay or erase what happened to us on October 7, what started this war: an attack on Israel by Hamas.

“I would have hoped that the media would respect our pain for just one day, the way they respect the pain of the people of Gaza all year round for the past two years.”

‘Deeply offensive’

Danny Cohen, the former director of BBC television, said: “This is shocking but not surprising. It is another example of the everyday, institutional bias at the BBC.

“To call the October 7 terrorist massacre an ‘escalation’ is deeply offensive. It is the kind of language Hamas might use.

“One might also assume that on this day the picture used by the BBC would focus on Jewish victims of the October 7 pogrom. But the BBC’s instinctive bias meant that this was not the case, even on this day of mourning.”

A BBC insider said: “It is shocking that such a description of a one-sided atrocity is used in, of all things, a briefing about audience expectations from the BBC. It demonstrates – again – unconscious bias and terrible insensitivity towards Jewish staff.”

After a number of staff complained about the wording and picture choice, the BBC apologised.

A spokesman for the broadcaster said: “This internal staff email should have been worded differently and we’re sorry for any offence caused. We are editing it and will replace the text on our intranet.”

It is understood that the email was written by a junior member of staff. 

A senior BBC news presenter told The Telegraph: “I don’t think the people who wrote that are evil, I think they just don’t understand anything about the modern world.

“To use a picture of Palestinian suffering when you are marking the second anniversary of the October 7 attacks: how does that get signed off? It just epitomises everything the BBC is having to grapple with internally.”

A BBC source said the email was illustrated with a picture of Gaza because it covered current audience attitudes to the Israel-Gaza conflict.

Robert Jenrick, the shadow justice secretary, said: “This is the latest shocking example of the bias within the BBC. When will they learn?”

Richard Tice, the deputy leader of Reform UK, accused the BBC of appearing “to be trying to deny the Oct 7 massacre” and of “rampant anti-Semitism”.

The email contained a link to a longer piece that referred to the attacks. It said: “On October 7th, 2023, the conflict between Palestine and Israel reached a new escalation when Hamas attacked southern Israel, killing 1,200 and taking over 200 hostages.

“In response, Israel launched a prolonged military campaign in Gaza, resulting in widespread destruction and a humanitarian crisis. Over the following months, ceasefire efforts stalled, global pressure intensified, and civilian casualties continued to rise, sparking worldwide protests and diplomatic tensions.

“In September 2025, the UK Government announced its formal recognition of the state of Palestine, shortly before the two-year anniversary of the Hamas attack.

“This moment presents both a challenge and an opportunity for BBC journalists in how they cover the conflict for our audiences.”

The BBC has previously faced accusations of anti-Israel bias. Tim Davie, the director-general, was forced to apologise to the Jewish community in July for broadcasting Bob Vylan’s anti-Semitic chants at Glastonbury.

Mr Davie’s leadership was called into question after the broadcaster failed to cut the live stream of the punk-rap duo urging the crowd to join them in chants calling for “death, death to the IDF”.

The BBC also failed to disclose to viewers that the child narrator of a Gaza documentary was the son of a Hamas minister, a breach which the corporation’s chairman, Samir Shah, described as “a dagger to the heart of the BBC’s claim to be impartial and trustworthy”.

data.title: It’s time banks invested in weapons, and General Sanders is just the man to do it
data.description:
In what might seem like a strange move, the Spanish Bank Santander has recruited one of the British Army’s finest leaders to enable the bank to ride the bow wave of the massive increase in defence spending across Europe. Some experts predict defence spending to top £500bn over the next 10 years, and that requires a lot of smart financing. After years of stagnation and depleted armies across Europe, President Putin has given a massive shot in the arm to the defence industries, with many British companies in the van of this battle. It’s past time our bankers got serious about financing new weapons and General Sanders is just the man to help.

Knowing Patrick Sanders, and knowing the defence industry, as an occasional specialist consultant since I left the Army, I’d gauge this as a pretty smart move. My feeling, having sat on both sides of the defence fence, is that the military instinctively think big business is trying to rip them off, and the commercial side thinks the military do not know what they want, and if they do, aspiration and resource seldom match.

This also comes at a time where the “wokerati” want nothing to do with the defence environment, hence some banks and investors have shied away from financing weaponry altogether, often because they want to score highly on rating systems which will mark them down for investing in arms firms or projects. Even among those who do invest in defence, there’s a strong tendency to steer clear of controversial weapons like land mines and cluster bombs. Nowadays, however, even the most liberally minded are finally realising that Putin and the Russian hordes are not going to be stopped by protestors waving placards and wearing fake keffiyehs. The explosion in the share values of companies like BAE and Thales, has no doubt encouraged investors.

It seems way beyond time that financial institutions should have engaged soldiers like Patrick Sanders to ensure the right money goes to the right places, and the right weapons are developed and delivered at pace to turn the Russian hordes back whence they came. I have felt a slight antipathy sometimes from commercial leaders to some of us ex-military Johnny-come-latelies in the defence industry, perhaps fearing we may take the top jobs. They have climbed the greasy pole of commerce while we were running up and down hills in Iraq and Afghanistan with not a financial thought in our minds. I get this, but I also realise that in any team you need balance, and I’m sure General Sanders is the right man for the job, at Santander at least.

Indeed many veterans are working in these burgeoning defence industries, but many seem pegged, to me at least, at levels well below their capabilities. I hope that this bold move will encourage other financial institutions – as well as defence and defence related industries – to make better use of the pool of veteran talent, most especially with war raging yet again in Europe.

One of Patrick Sanders’ last major speeches before he stood down as Chief of the General Staff was to tell the Government and the country to “prepare for war”. The leaders of Western Europe heard him, though the actions taken so far have mostly been cautious. When Putin looked west on 24 Feb 2022, he did not see a conventional Nato deterrent to stop him attacking Ukraine. Most of the experts who actually understand his motives believe he will keep pushing westwards until he does see that.

General Sanders and his fellow financiers now must ensure that enough money gets to the right places to provide that credible conventional deterrence in Europe. I judge he’ll do it, in spades.

data.title: AstraZeneca threatens to stop investing in Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca’s UK chairman has threatened to halt investment in Britain unless the Government backs down over an NHS drug pricing row.

Shaun Grady warned that Britain was in danger of disappearing from a list of attractive global destinations unless it brings its “appalling” drug pricing regime into the 21st century.

Speaking at the Conservative Party conference, Mr Grady said the UK “fell down” on adopting innovation with patients being denied cutting-edge drug treatments as a result.

He blamed a lack of government support for new projects and an agreement that places a cap on how much the NHS spends on branded medicines for putting future investment at risk.

AstraZeneca announced last month that it was putting on hold a planned £200m upgrade of its Cambridge research site. The company had earlier axed a £450m expansion of its vaccine manufacturing plant in Liverpool after Labour cut funding for the project.

Mr Grady said: “We’ve [the UK] failed to keep pace on medicine investment over the years.”

Asked what it would take for the company to go ahead with the Cambridge investment, he said: “Unless the commercial environment changes, the UK is not going to be on the list.”

Mr Grady added that the company would “revisit and reflect” on its Cambridge plans as talks with the Government continue.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers this summer after failing to strike a new pricing deal on medicines supplied to the NHS. It is understood that talks are still ongoing, led by Lord Vallance, the science minister.

While Mr Grady said AstraZeneca remained a significant investor in the UK, he said it was missing out on vital investment because of an antiquated system used by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) to determine which treatments are available on the NHS that has largely remained the same since 1999.

This is determined by a formula which looks at drug effectiveness in terms of how much it would cost to give a person a year of healthy life.

“The thresholds have not been changed for 25 years, which is pretty appalling,” said Mr Grady.

He added that, unlike the UK, countries including Ireland and Singapore were offering companies significant subsidies or tax breaks to invest there. He also compared Britain with the US, where the company announced in July it would invest £50bn by 2030.

“This is a competitive global pitch,” said Mr Grady. “We announced a £50bn investment in the US. We met the governor of Virginia in our offices in London, we announced that £50bn investment 33 days later. We need to think about that speed and what truly is a concierge service.”

Speaking on the same panel, Lord Harrington – who led a review on investment under the last Tory government – said a lack of joined-up thinking was also stymieing growth.

He said: “There’s a room full of civil servants in the Department of Health whose job it is to basically kick the c--- out of pharmaceutical companies to get the price that the government pays for drugs as low as possible. They are not allowed to consider holistically the effect of those decisions on investment in a country, whereas a company clearly does.”

The decision by AstraZeneca, the UK’s largest listed company, means all of its £650m investment package announced in 2024 risks being shelved.

The Department of Health launched a review of so-called NHS rebates earlier this year under pressure from Donald Trump, the US president, who has demanded that pharmaceutical companies lower their drug prices in the US – where they are much higher than the UK and Europe.

A voluntary agreement currently places a cap on how much the NHS spends on branded medicines as part of efforts to keep costs down.

The total bill is allowed to rise by a certain percentage each year. If the NHS spends more than expected on branded medicines, it is able to claw some of that money back in rebates on drugs.

The Government raised the rate unexpectedly to almost 23pc for 2025 for newer medicines at the end of last year.

Pharmaceutical bosses have warned the scheme is preventing the launch of cutting-edge medicines in the UK. They have pressed the UK to cut the rate of rebates into single digits, a level seen elsewhere in Europe.

HS2 will almost double long-distance rail capacity between London and the West Midlands, the Government has said, and that this should in turn free up space on existing lines for more passenger and freight services.

It added that work is ongoing on possible changes to service pattern, infrastructure and rolling stock to eke out additional capacity, especially north of Birmingham, though there is no question of reversing the decision to cancel phase 2 of HS2

A spokesman for the Department for Transport said: “No decisions have been made on HS2’s future timetable, so any suggestion of reduced capacity is pure speculation.”

A Government spokesman said: “Through the Life Sciences Sector Plan, we are making the UK the destination of choice for business, investing up to £600 million in the Health Data Research Service with the Wellcome Trust, committing up to £520m to the Life Sciences Innovative Manufacturing Fund and welcoming billions of UK investment from companies like Moderna and BioNTech.

“We will always put patients and taxpayers first, encouraging investment and innovation whilst ensuring best value for money. 

“Having made an unprecedented £1bn through the Voluntary Scheme on branded medicines to industry, we will continue to engage.”

data.title: Put aside your ego and you’ll see downsizing gives you freedom
data.description:
In a week of gloom for the property market – Kirstie Allsopp says “it’s dead” and the papers are full of tales of woe from those with expensive houses that have been on the market for years – I offer a differing view.

In January we decided to downsize. All of this year we’ve been trying to sell the house we’ve lived in for 23 years; the family home where we’ve raised our children, sent them to school, seen them take their first steps and now punted them off to university and beyond.

It’s been emotional. First the decision to do it. “You can only sell the family home once,” warned an older friend. She’s right, it’s hard to part with the walls which have witnessed so many happy memories. But I also think it is crazy to stay in a house for the sake of Christmas dinner. So many families keep a huge barn of a place they no longer need just to be able to muster the clan at key moments. Isn’t that what an Airbnb is for?

We decided to downsize to free up some cash. Like many Britons, our family house is currently the custodian of all our financial Lego chips. We’ve almost paid off the mortgage, but we realised that even if we do, all our money will still be in the house (and with the prospect of Rachel Reeves imposing capital gains tax on primary residences, it felt like a good time to sell).

It’s also the right time. We have one child about to finish university and the other out in the world: do we really need a four-bedroom family house? When the children aren’t there we fairly rattle around. It costs a fortune to heat the big, empty rooms, particularly as energy prices have soared. And we’re in the catchment area for several excellent state schools, none of which we need anymore. It’s a brilliant house for raising a family, but that isn’t us anymore. Our children are raised. It’s time to move on, to clear out so that someone else can benefit from the house and its position: the place needs the patter of tiny feet, the rhythm of children going to school and coming home. We bought it when I was four months pregnant with our first child – she’s about to turn 23.

The trouble is that while it makes sense to sell up, to reorganise our finances into different, more accessible piles, it is also hard to do. We’ve spent months on a RightMove binge – usually in bed, both of us clicking and swiping and comparing. We looked around nearly 40 flats, eventually deciding we needed three bedrooms, in case the kids boomerang home; but in their absence the rooms can be an office and a snug. We couldn’t afford three bedrooms and a garden. That is a compromise.

Finding looking at London flats depressing, we considered moving out of the capital. You can buy a palace in the country for the price of a two-bed in London. So we started playing Escape to the Country for real – the space, the garden – until the reality of being hours from family, friends, community, work and culture brought us back to our senses. We’re staying in inner London for now; it’s my happy place. But the countryside fantasy made me realise that much of my reluctance to downsize was wrapped up with status. By moving out we could have a bigger, cheaper house. But the reality of downsizing is exactly that: living somewhere smaller and less swanky. Looking round all those flats, I’ve realised that there is a lot of ego and status involved in where we decide to live. If we’re going effectively to right-size (my preferred term to downsizing, which feels depressing and regressive), we need to get over ourselves and our status anxiety and look to the bigger picture.

The truth is, we don’t need all our current space, and before we lived in this big house we were totally happy in smaller flats. This move is about right-organising our lives now that we are in our mid-50s. That involves swapping real-estate square-footage and our glorious floor-to-ceiling windows for a smaller flat which has enough space for us all but also leaves us mortgage-free with a bit left over. That means freedom; if it all goes through (fingers crossed we are moving next month), my husband can retire and I’ll only have to do the work that excites me. I am aware that is a hugely privileged position to be in.

Taking the ego hit on the house means less stress, more pleasure and leisure and the opportunity to help our fledgling adult children by paying for Masters degrees (increasingly necessary to get them on the jobs ladder) or to act as the bank of Mum and Dad if they want to get on the housing market themselves in due course. In the meantime there is still space for all of us in the new pad.

The last year has been a lesson not just in real estate but in working out what success looks like to us now. It’s been a big shift. I’ve moved from feeling that my nice house which I’ve worked hard to pay for defines me, to realising that success for me now isn’t a status abode but the freedom to live the life I want. I’ve learnt that getting over my real-estate ego equals freedom. That the smaller flat – yes, we finally found something that fit our criteria of having something nice about it and three bedrooms, just around the corner from where we live now but for half the money – means liberation from monthly payments and having to work to meet them.

There’s a compromise; our new flat definitely does not have the wow factor of our old house. It’s on a busier road, it’s not as pretty; Hampstead Heath will have to be our garden.

I am writing this because, as a property columnist for many years, I saw so many people who were trapped, Miss Havisham-like, in houses that no longer fitted them; clinging to the past. Yes, change is difficult, and scary. But taking the plunge, selling up, deciding to define success differently and live in a new way is exciting and rejuvenating.

Change, after all, is evidence of life. It’s not only good for you, but helps the next generation by freeing up family homes near good schools. And yes, we are in the process of a massive clear-out (clothes, books, old toys, the detritus of a quarter of a century), which is hard, but we are getting there. And success for me now is about freedom, not a status house.

So what would right-sizing look like for you? Remember, freedom is success: success is freedom. What are you waiting for?

data.title: Millions of households to face even higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility company to increase their charges.

Following a ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, which is lower than the £649 requested, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495, which is lower than the £515 the company had asked for.

South East Water had asked for an increase of up to £322 but this was reduced to £286, while Southern’s request of £710 annual bills was rejected in favour of £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622, down from its requested £642.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money will help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it will help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said: “I understand the public’s anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay.

“We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

data.title: Billionaire Revolut founder abandons Britain for UAE
data.description:
Revolut’s billionaire chief executive has quit London for the United Arab Emirates, joining the growing exodus of wealthy entrepreneurs from the UK.

Nikolay Storonsky, the founder of the $75bn (£56bn) fintech giant and one of Britain’s richest men, has moved to the Gulf state, according to a filing for his family office.

In an embarrassment for the Government, the move comes just two weeks after Rachel Reeves hailed the opening of Revolut’s new London office as a vote of confidence in Britain.

Mr Storonsky, 41, was born in Russia but founded Revolut in the UK in 2015. The company’s latest share sale will value his stake in the business at around £13.4bn.

Despite founding and growing the company in London, Mr Storonsky has been a frequent critic of the business environment in the UK.

In particular, he has criticised regulators over delays to the company gaining a UK banking licence and suggested he would not list the company in London.

A filing on Tuesday from Storonsky Family Ltd, his family office, listed the UAE as his “new country or state usually resident”. The filing also said that the founder of the fintech giant had moved there last year.

The company had previously denied reports that Mr Storonsky had moved to Dubai, saying: “Nik is based in and registered to Revolut’s UK entity.”

He is already known to spend much of his time in the country.

The finance app recently announced plans to invest £3bn in Britain and create 1,000 jobs over the next five years at an event attended by the Chancellor. At the event, Mr Storonsky said the UK was the company’s “number one market”.

Mubadala, Abu Dhabi’s sovereign wealth fund, is already an investor in Revolut and is in talks to buy a further $100m stake in the company, according to reports. Revolut recently secured a payments licence in the UAE and has outlined plans to expand in the country.

At a Dubai summit last year, Mr Storonsky praised the city’s “advanced infrastructure and investor-friendly policies” as well as its growth as a financial hub, according to a government announcement.

His relocation comes amid growing concerns that a large number of millionaires are fleeing Britain, fuelled by Labour’s decision to tighten inheritance tax rules and abolish non-dom status. The UAE, by contrast, imposes no income tax.

Prominent business figures to leave the UK since Labour’s tax changes were announced include the Goldman Sachs banker Richard Gnodde, and John Reece, the finance chief of chemicals giant Ineos.

The Chancellor has considered reversing elements of her tax raid, in particular a 40pc inheritance tax on global assets.

As well as attending the opening of Revolut’s new London office, Ms Reeves has been lobbying the company to list on the London Stock Exchange. It would mark a significant coup and provide a shot in the arm to the market after a long stretch of struggling to secure listings.

Revolut is reportedly considering a dual listing in the US and UK, despite Mr Storonsky having previously said it would not be “rational” to list in Britain because of stamp duty on buying shares.

The Chancellor is considering introducing a stamp duty holiday for newly-listed companies as a way of attracting more companies to float in the UK.

Ms Reeves has also sought to speed up the approval of Revolut’s banking licence. The company was granted provisional status last summer, and the Chancellor sought to arrange a meeting to speed full approval, which prompted a clash with the Bank of England.

Securing a UK banking licence is seen as crucial to the company obtaining similar credentials elsewhere around the world.

Mr Storonsky moved to the UK in 2004 and later became a British citizen, working for Lehman Brothers and Credit Suisse before founding Revolut.

He renounced his Russian citizenship in 2022 after Vladimir Putin’s invasion of Ukraine.

Revolut was contacted for comment.

data.title: Starmer pledges to spend billions more on drugs in NHS overhaul
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer is preparing to boost NHS spending on drugs to prevent an exodus of investment from the UK.

The Government is set to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. The move will allow the NHS to pay billions of pounds more for medicines.

Several drug companies have scrapped investments in the UK amid a row over how much the Government was spending on new drugs compared to other countries.

The pricing shake-up will appease drugmakers – but it also threatens to put Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, on a collision course with Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, over how to fund the multibillion-pound bill.

Lord Vallance, the science minister – who is negotiating a new deal with the industry on behalf of the Government – admitted last month that price increases were necessary to stop a wave of investment leaving the UK.

The Times reported that the Treasury is insisting that spending increases must be funded through existing departmental budgets, while NHS bosses are demanding extra financial support to pay for any deal.

The looming showdown comes as the Chancellor prepares to unveil another tax-raising Budget, with economists predicting extra levies of around £30bn to balance the books.

Ms Reeves boosted health spending at the expense of other government departments in her June spending review, with the department accounting for 90pc of the extra money for public services over the next three years.

It means the NHS and other health spending is now on course to account for half of all spending on public services by the end of the decade.

Meanwhile, a string of companies including Merck, the US cancer drug manufacturer, and Eli Lily, maker of obesity jab Mounjaro, have halted investment in the UK over the past year in protest against the NHS’s drug pricing regime.

This week, AstraZeneca’s UK chairman signalled that the company was ready to scrap investment in Britain unless the Government backed down on pricing.

Shaun Grady warned that the UK was in danger of becoming unattractive for companies unless it brings its “appalling” drugs regime into the 21st century.

Pharmaceutical companies are also under pressure from Donald Trump, the US president, who has escalated tensions by threatening 100pc tariffs on the sector if it does not invest in America.

The antiquated system used by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) to determine which treatments are available on the NHS has not been changed since 1999.

The system uses a formula which seeks to determine value for money based on a drug’s effectiveness.

Currently, Nice will approve medicines that cost less than £20,000-£30,000 for every extra “quality-adjusted life year”.

A new range of between £25,000-£35,000 per year is said to be one option under consideration.

However, the Association of the British Pharmaceutical Industry has warned that the threshold would be closer to £56,000 if it had climbed in line with the cost of living. It has called for a range closer to £50,000.

A government spokesman said: “The pharmaceutical sector and the innovative medicines it produces are critical to our NHS, our economy and the plan for change.

“Through our life sciences sector plan, we’ve committed to working with industry to accelerate growth in spending on innovative medicines compared to the previous decade.

“We’ve secured a landmark economic partnership with the US that includes working together on pharmaceutical exports from the UK whilst improving conditions for pharmaceutical companies here.

“We’re now in advanced discussions with the US administration to secure the best outcome for the UK, reflecting our strong relationship and the opportunities from close partnership with our pharmaceutical industry.”

data.title: Passengers face a decade of delays on London-Scotland rail route
data.description:
Millions of passengers face diversions of two hours or more during a decade of engineering work on Britain’s busiest intercity railway line.

A project to renew 1970s-era overhead wires and signalling equipment on the London to Glasgow route will commence in August next year, Network Rail said.

The work will bring years of disruption to 200 miles of the West Coast Main Line (WCML) north of Crewe as sections of track are closed for weeks at a time.

An indication of the likely impact was provided on Monday when train operator Avanti West Coast said the replacement of a bridge on the line will lead to two and a half-hour detours between Preston to Carlisle for two weeks in January.

The work on the Clifton Bridge, which spans the M6 in Cumbria, will see trains diverted off the 125mph main line onto the remote and non-electrified Settle and Carlisle route, which normally has just eight local passenger trains a day in each direction.

It will stretch the 80-mile journey from Preston to Carlisle to one of almost 120 miles, adding 80 minutes to the usual travel time, excluding a longer wait at either end as the service is expected to run only every two hours.

Avanti said it will use the detour to prepare for the years of regular closures beginning later in 2026.

Avanti, which is due to be nationalised in October 2026, said the re-routing will depend on the areas of the WCML that are closed, as well as factors such as the time of year and anticipated passenger loads.

It said the work will deliver a “once-in-a-generation” modernisation that will drive up train performance and boost economic growth.

Phil James, Network Rail’s route director for the North West, said the diversions will allow more people to complete their journeys without transferring to cars and buses, though rail replacement buses will still be required to reach Lancaster and two stations in Cumbria.

“We work together as a rail industry to keep passengers on trains and limit disruption as much as possible during our planned engineering upgrades,” he said.

January’s work will see a 60-year-old bridge over which only one train can pass at a time replaced with a 500-foot, 3,000-tonne structure costing £60m.

In a taste of things to come, the company said people travelling from north-west England will have to choose between making the detour or taking a slow train across the Pennines to reach Scotland by way of the East Coast Mine Line.

Passengers wanting to travel to Glasgow from London will be advised to abandon the WCML completely and use the East Coast line throughout, extending the trip to almost five hours, as will those from Birmingham, via a lengthy journey across the Midlands.

While Avanti runs the most trains on the WCML, carrying 35 million passengers in the year through March, the northern reaches of the route are also used by operators including Northern, TransPennine, ScotRail, Transport for Wales and the Caledonian Sleeper.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Hit drivers with pay-per-mile tax to fix Budget, Reeves urged
data.description:
Drivers should be taxed for every mile they travel to help Rachel Reeves fix a hole in Britain’s public finances, a leading think tank has urged.

The Resolution Foundation, which has considerable influence in Labour circles, said vehicle taxes should be overhauled to replace lost fuel duty as more drivers switch from petrol and diesel cars to electric vehicles (EVs).

The think tank has proposed a system to hit drivers with an annual tax plus another fee of between 3p and 9p per mile driven. Drivers of heavier vehicles could be charged the higher price, reflecting wear and tear on Britain’s roads.

“Motoring taxes are an important part of the tax system but they are also an obvious and significant fiscal risk,” said Adam Corlett, who wrote the report.

It’s feared the Chancellor will have to raise as much as £30bn in taxes in her November Budget.

Taxing drivers per mile could raise up to £20bn per year for the Government, the Resolution Foundation suggested.

Fuel duty revenues are set to fall by one fifth by the end of this decade compared to five years ago, from £28bn to £22.6bn, according to the Office for Budget Responsibility.

That drop comes as drivers switch to EVs as well as to hybrid cars and more efficient traditional petrol and diesel models.

Mr Corlett suggested the lightest cars be charged 3p, rising to 9p per mile for heavier models.

“Distance could be logged via a mixture of MOTs, self-reporting and ideally telematics (with the latter helping to exclude miles driven outside the UK and perhaps facilitating local per-mile additions in future to tackle the worst congestion),” his report said.

He also proposes cutting VAT on public chargers from 20pc to 5pc to match the tax on home electricity bills as well as increasing taxes on non-electric cars.

The Resolution Foundation estimates the changes would raise £2bn per year by the end of the decade, £10bn in 2035 and £20bn per year by 2040.

Motoring groups oppose road pricing. Ian Taylor, from the Alliance of British Drivers, said pay per mile schemes are not “particularly fair”.

“It would almost certainly put prices up and probably the only way to administer it is to track everyone, which has freedom and privacy implications,” he said, noting options include roadside cameras or satellites.

“It costs a lot of money to administer, regardless of how you do it.”

Mr Corlett also proposed ending the freeze on fuel duty, which has not increased since 2011. He said the tax should rise by 3pc every year.

Similarly the temporary 5p cut, which is due to expire in March, could be reversed gradually over a number of years to ease the impact “when concern about inflation is likely to still be high”.

This change would see the tax rise from 52.95p per litre currently to almost 70p by the end of the decade.

Petrol cost an average of 134p per litre last week, according to the Department for Transport. That is up by just over 6pc from around 126p before the pandemic – a noticeable increase for drivers.

A Treasury spokesman said: “The Chancellor makes tax policy decisions at fiscal events. We do not comment on speculation around future changes to tax policy.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.

data.title: Ryanair accused of ‘disgraceful’ attitude to older passengers
data.description:
Ryanair has been accused of ignoring elderly passengers after confirming plans to scrap paper boarding passes.

A Ryanair shake-up, due to take effect in November, will result in 40 million Ryanair customers who use paper tickets being forced to carry passes on a smartphone, which campaigners said will make travelling harder for older passengers.

Silver Voices, an over-60s lobby group, branded the decision a “disgraceful move” and said the low-cost airline was putting profit ahead of the well-being of customers.

Age UK said it will disadvantage the four million Britons who have never used the internet and the millions more who lack an Android or Apple mobile phone.

On Wednesday, Ryanair said it will adopt digital-only ticketing from Nov 12, meaning “passengers will no longer be able to download and print a physical paper boarding pass”.

It said that travellers “will instead need to use the digital boarding pass generated in their ‘myRyanair’ app during check-in to board their Ryanair flight”.

Ryanair, led by Michael O’Leary, will become the first major European airline to ban paper passes, which are still accepted at British Airways, easyJet, Jet2, Wizz Air and TUI.

Impact on customers

Dennis Reed, director of Silver Voices, accused Ryanair of seeking to “cut costs and cut corners without caring about the impact on their customers”.

He said: “It’s a disgraceful move. They are effectively saying they don’t want older people as passengers. There’s a strong argument to say that it’s discriminatory.”

He argued that many older people do not have smartphones, while those who do may not know how to download an app or have the physical capacity to manipulate a small device, whether due to eyesight issues, arthritis or some form of disability.

He added: “This is going to cause chaos. If somebody goes to the airport to catch a Ryanair flight without the app, what are they going to do? Turn them away?

“It’s also going to isolate older people from society, which is what businesses that go completely digital do.

“I really would urge them to reconsider. I, for one, will not be flying with Ryanair, and I would urge other people to take the same action.”

Caroline Abrahams, charity director at Age UK, said companies should not ignore those with limited digital skills when updating their ticketing processes.

She said: “There should always be an alternative way of booking and showing tickets that does not disadvantage those who aren’t online.”

Ryanair originally planned to scrap paper boarding passes over the summer but shelved the idea amid concerns that it would create confusion and delays at the busiest time for travel.

The airline later said the change would take effect on Nov 3, but has pushed it back by a further nine days to avoid introducing it during the busy half-term school break.

Dara Brady, Ryanair’s chief marketing officer, said the new timing should “ensure a seamless transition” to 100pc digital boarding passes, which he said would mean “a faster, smarter and greener travel experience for our customers”.

Ryanair said the switch should also eliminate almost all airport check-in fees, as passengers will need to check in online or via the app to generate their digital pass.

It said that almost 80pc of its 200 million passengers already use digital boarding passes, and that going paper-free will save over 300 tonnes in waste each year.

The airline claimed that for passengers with impaired vision, the app has the advantage of gathering information in one place, with screen readers able to read the details aloud – something it said “cannot be completed with outdated paper boarding passes”.

It said passengers who have checked in and downloaded a boarding pass will be assisted free of charge at the airport if their phone subsequently dies or is lost.

data.title: Northern Powerhouse Rail to be delayed again in blow to Starmer
data.description:
Northern Powerhouse Rail is to be delayed again, in a blow to Sir Keir Starmer.

Concerns over the cost of the plan to build a rail line between Liverpool and Manchester have led it to be dropped from the Prime Minister’s conference speech.

It had been set to be the flagship announcement at Labour’s annual gathering in Liverpool next week.

Sources told the BBC, which first reported the delay, that it was an attempt to avoid similar mistakes to those made by HS2, which has repeatedly gone over budget.

Officials were said to be particularly concerned about whether connecting Manchester airport to the city centre, a part of Northern Powerhouse Rail that overlapped with part of the cancelled HS2 phase 2, still represented value for money.

Sir Keir had been hoping to use his conference speech to relaunch his premiership after a torrid summer.

He has lost Angela Rayner, former deputy prime minister, after a Telegraph investigation into her tax affairs, while Lord Mandelson, his ambassador to the US, was forced to resign over his links to Jeffrey Epstein.

Morgan McSweeney, his most trusted adviser, is also facing questions over late payments from a think tank he helped set up to the Electoral Commission.

Andy Burnham, whom some Labour MPs are urging to challenge Sir Keir for the Labour leadership, has long been a champion of the project in his job as Manchester mayor.

In response to the plans being delayed again, a source close to Burnham told the BBC: “Any more tyre kicking and there will be no tyre left.”

Northern Powerhouse Rail was initially floated by George Osborne in 2014 as a means of rebalancing the economy, and was projected to cost around £43bn.

Initial plans would have seen the line stretching between Newcastle, Hull, Sheffield and Liverpool, linking to the since-cancelled eastern spur of HS2.

Revisions by Jeremy Hunt in 2022 saw the plans cut back to a new, £17bn line between Liverpool and Manchester.

In June, Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, said she would “set out” plans to “take forward our ambitions for Northern Powerhouse Rail… in the coming weeks”.

Since the spending review, a black hole of up to £50bn has emerged in the public finances, which she is expected to fill with a swathe of tax rises.

The Labour-linked Resolution Foundation think tank urged the Chancellor earlier this week to cut National Insurance by 2p and add it to income tax.

Another suggestion from the Resolution Foundation, whose former chief executive Torsten Bell is now a Treasury minister after being selected for a safe Labour seat in 2024, is a pay-per-mile tax on motorists.

A Conservative spokesman said: “Yet again it’s more big talk, and no real plans from this Labour Government.

“By bowing down to their union paymasters with inflation-busting pay rises Labour sent the signal that their donors come first, and passengers second – and the fiasco of nationalisation has been proof of that.”

Paul Kohler, the Liberal Democrats’ transport spokesman, said: “This setback delays the vital boost to northern communities that they have been promised.

“The Government must move quickly to resolve these issues, stop the delays and get on with the job. At the moment, this project is just as plagued by delays as actual trains.”

A government source said ministers remain “fully committed to Northern Powerhouse Rail”.

“We are determined to learn from the mistakes of the past and not repeat the previous government’s failures over HS2 and the botched Network North plan.

“That’s why we’re taking the time to get this right. We will set out our plans in the coming weeks”, they said.

data.title: A ‘taxi tax’ will only hurt the vulnerable. Labour has truly lost the plot
data.description:
“Policies like the taxi tax,” Charlie Gilkes tells me, “destroy the ecosystem in which the hospitality sector functions”.

Mr Gilkes, the co-founder of the Inception Group which owns bars and restaurants across London, explains why it isn’t just the taxi industry which is on high alert following rumours that VAT will be added to all cab journeys.

Hospitality was dealt a devastating blow by the Chancellor’s Budget last year, particularly with the change in employers’ National Insurance contributions. In the nine months that followed the industry lost 89,000 jobs, according to the trade body UKHospitality, which is nearly half of all job losses seen in the wider economy.

A 1.2pc rise in NICs means that the earnings threshold at which employers start to be charged dropped from £9,100 to £5,000 a year, bringing even very low-paid part-time employees into the remit of this tax for the first time.

It is not difficult to see why this policy has hit the industry, which is “a leading creator of part-time jobs, first jobs for young people, older people and people returning to the jobs market”, especially hard.

Against what is already a very challenging backdrop, a taxi tax would hit hospitality businesses even harder, right across the nation. Country publicans are concerned about higher taxi fares putting off patrons. Staff members, particularly women, often rely on taxi shares in the absence of night buses. These workers will face a rise in the cost of their journey home after a late shift.

A large proportion of those who use taxis are vulnerable people, Layla Barke-Jones, a partner at Aaron and Partners Solicitors tells me. They could be the elderly who are unable to drive themselves or those with a disability who find it difficult to use public transport. “These are the people who would be disproportionately hit,” Ms Barke-Jones warns.

Does this mean local authorities – who are increasingly facing scrutiny for their use of private taxis for residents or even asylum seekers – would have to pay the taxi tax too, passing on an additional burden on to taxpayers?

According to Jonathan Main, a VAT expert and partner at professional services firm MHA, the answer to that question, thankfully, is no. Local authorities belong to a separate VAT regime, which allows them to recover any VAT costs incurred in the process of carrying out their statutory duties.

However, if the taxi tax pushes up the cost of cab journeys, not just by the 20pc levied in VAT, but considerably more than that as operators and taxi drivers grapple with the resulting administrative costs, then it’s difficult to see how that extra cost would not be passed on to all consumers.

And that brings us to the group whose livelihoods would be the most directly threatened by this rumoured tax: taxi drivers themselves.

A spokesman for Bolt, the taxi operating service, says: “They are self-employed entrepreneurs, and around 70pc work outside London. A flat 20pc VAT would inevitably raise costs for consumers and reduce demand, leading to a drop in driver earnings.

“We believe VAT should be applied fairly on operators’ margins [as is already the case], not on the full fare, to protect drivers’ livelihoods and keep rides affordable for passengers.”

It is clear that the Budget in late November will be the frostiest yet. A ruinous taxi tax will only prolong the winter.

data.title: GSK boss says US is ‘biggest priority’ as Trump pressures drugmakers
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drugmaker has said the US is its “biggest priority” amid pressure from Donald Trump to slash prices for US citizens.

Dame Emma Walmsley, who will step down from GSK at the end of the year, praised the US and said the group is working with the Trump administration after it launched a campaign to address high drug prices.

Shares in pharmaceutical companies surged on Tuesday after rival pharma group Pfizer struck a deal with the White House to lower prices for some of its medicines. Mr Trump has also demanded greater investment in the US from drug companies after introducing a 100pc tariff on branded drugs made abroad.

The Pfizer agreement, which averts tariffs for three years, triggered a relief rally that sent shares in AstraZeneca up 11.2pc, as it reclaimed its place as Britain’s most valuable company. GSK shares jumped by 6.2pc.

Dame Emma admitted GSK was “betting big on the US” after she pledged during Mr Trump’s UK state visit last month to invest $30bn (£22bn) in America over the next five years.

She said: “Absolutely GSK is engaging constructively. The markets also view it this way, as it’s about constructive dialogue between the industry and the Government.

“The US is by far the biggest priority market for our industry because the US is the best country in the world to discover and develop innovation.”

As the country’s largest drugmaker, GSK has become a national champion for the UK pharmaceutical sector, despite generating only a small proportion of its revenues in the UK.

Britain represents just 2pc of the £65bn drugmaker sales, compared to 52pc in the US.

Despite this, Dame Emma said the group would not ditch its listing on the London stock market for New York. GSK’s shares have fallen overall in the eight years since she took over in 2017.

“I’m not getting distracted by the listing questions that everyone keeps asking about,” Dame Emma told Bloomberg’s Women, Money & Power conference.

“Our listing location doesn’t distract from where we allocate our capital in terms of investments. There are no plans for that.”

She also said there had been “zero engagement” on a potential merger with any other UK pharmaceutical company.

AstraZeneca fears

Her comments come days after AstraZeneca stoked fears that Britain’s biggest drugmaker could quit London after it announced plans to list shares on the New York Stock Exchange.

In a shake-up announced on Monday, the FTSE pharmaceuticals giant said it would upgrade its US listing to ensure it is equally as important in New York as it is on the London Stock Exchange.

Bosses said the move would make it easier for the company to raise money from US investors, describing it as “a global listing for global investors in a global company”.

Analysts at AJ Bell said the decision hinted “at the possibility of a more dramatic shift at some point in the future”.

data.title: Runaway oil tycoon re-emerges to deny knowledge of £1.5bn debt pile
data.description:
The runaway boss of a failed oil refinery has re-emerged to claim he does not know how much the company owes to creditors.

Winston Soosaipillai, who ran Lindsey oil refinery owner Prax, said he cannot provide details of the company’s £1.5bn debt pile, nor does he know how much money the company still has after its collapse earlier this year.

In his first public commentary since the downfall of his energy empire, Mr Soosaipillai told Prax’s administrators: “I am without the direct knowledge necessary for concurring with the outstanding balances owed to creditors.”

That is despite his role as chairman and chief executive of Prax before its collapse. He was also the company’s owner alongside his wife, Arani Soosaipillai.

Instead, he told the High Court that he was solely reliant on the financial information provided by Ben Lahnstein, the company’s chief financial officer.

The admission was made in a so-called statement of truth signed by Mr Soosaipillai earlier this month. He also said he was unable to confirm “the values of the company’s assets”.

It is the first time Mr Soosaipillai has made any comments regarding Prax’s failure, which has put hundreds of jobs at risk and raised concerns over Britain’s fuel supplies.

Authorities had previously been unable to contact Mr Soosaipillai in the wake of his company’s demise, with calls and emails going unanswered. His whereabouts have largely been unknown.

Administrators were drafted to oversee Prax’s unexpected failure in June. At the time Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, launched an urgent investigation and said he was concerned about “the conduct of directors”.

The administrators of Prax have since issued a lawsuit against Mr Soosaipillai – in which they have accused him of deceit and misrepresentation. Court documents have also claimed a breach of contract and raised concerns about a loan facility.

A report into the company’s demise on Companies House recently blamed “material irregularities” that were discovered in a £783m loan agreement with HSBC.

While Mr Soosaipillai has sought to keep a low profile since putting hundreds of jobs at risk, The Telegraph previously revealed that the oil tycoon still owned a £5m property in Surrey.

A long list of creditors is owed vast sums following Prax’s collapse, including £250m to HMRC. High-profile creditors include Shell – which is owed £9.2m – as well as Uber and Rentokil.

The complex nature of Mr Soosaipillai’s business empire means hundreds of millions of pounds are also owed to some of his other energy companies, which include petrol stations and other oil and gas operations.

Administrators are now trying to unpick the company’s remaining assets to see how much can be distributed to the creditors, if anything. In correspondence to the administrators, Mr Lahnstein has suggested that this will amount to at least £11.8m.

Union chiefs recently warned that the site is at risk of a “catastrophe” following an exodus of workers.

In a letter to Mr Miliband, Unite the Union said the site – which is currently under the control of the Government’s insolvency arm – is in an “unsafe and dangerous state”.

The Lindsey refinery was previously part of the wider Prax Group, which includes some 200 petrol stations plus oil and gas operations west of the Shetland Islands.

It supplied petrol, diesel, fuel oil, kerosene, aviation fuel and bitumen, and Mr Soosaipillai purchased the facility from French oil giant Total in 2021.

The administrators declined to comment.
