data.title: Jaguar Land Rover to restart car production after cyber attack
data.description:
Jaguar Land Rover (JLR) is to restart full production following a cyber attack that forced the car maker into an outage lasting more than five weeks.

The company said it will begin calling workers back to their posts in the West Midlands from Wednesday, as well as those working on production in Slovakia.

Plants producing engines in Wolverhampton and batteries in North Warwickshire will restart operations on Wednesday, along with other stages of production in Castle Bromwich, Halewood and Solihull.

Full production of Range Rover and Range Rover Sport cars in Solihull and of Defender and Discovery cars in Nitra, Slovakia, will then follow later in the week, JLR said.

There is no confirmation yet for when the company’s Halewood plant, in Merseyside, will resume production. JLR currently manufactures the Discovery Sport and Range Rover Evoque models at the site, but has previously announced plans for it to become its first all-electric production hub.

However, the company had delayed the launch of its first electric Range Rover before the cyber attack and the impact of the subsequent production outage is currently unclear.

To support suppliers who have gone weeks without revenues, some of which are on the verge of collapse, JLR also said it would begin making early payments for components.

Adrian Mardell, chief executive, said: “This week marks an important moment for JLR and all our stakeholders as we now restart our manufacturing operations following the cyber incident.

“Our suppliers are central to our success, and today we are launching a new financing arrangement that will enable us to pay our suppliers early, using the strength of our balance sheet to support their cash flows.

“I would like to thank everyone connected to JLR for their commitment, hard work and endeavour in recent weeks to bring us to this moment. We know there is much more to do, but our recovery is firmly under way.”

JLR’s production has been shut down since Aug 31 after hackers launched a crippling cyber attack on the company.

When the intrusion was discovered, JLR shut down all of its global systems to stem the damage, causing havoc throughout the business, with showrooms, repair shops and factories unable to carry out normal operations.

The hack also had a knock-on effect on hundreds of businesses that supply JLR with components, forcing some to lay off workers and seek to stave off collapse.

On Tuesday, the company said it was launching a finance scheme to provide suppliers who met certain criteria “with cash up front during the production restart phase”.

This week, it will resume automatic invoice payments after being forced to revert to manual payments during the shutdown.

JLR said it was working with banks to run the financing scheme, which would initially apply only to production suppliers.

Peter Kyle, the Business and Trade Secretary, said: “This is very welcome news for workers and suppliers but I know many are still under pressure, particularly further down the supply chain.

“My focus will remain on helping JLR resolve this cyber incident, making further progress towards restarting production and supporting the long-term health of our automotive supply chain.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain warned of tight energy supplies this winter
data.description:
Britain will import electricity from Europe to keep the lights on this winter as grid operators warn of tight energy supplies over the coming months.

The National Energy System Operator (Neso), which manages the power system, said supplies would come under most strain in early December and mid-January, particularly on cold, calmer days, when output from wind farms falls.

But it also insisted the grid was “secure and resilient” with new gas power stations, batteries and interconnector cables with France and other countries helping to provide the biggest safety cushion since 2020.

In its latest “winter outlook”, Neso has forecast that the capacity margin – the amount of untapped power still available – during cold spells will be about 6.1 gigawatts this winter, or about 10pc of demand.

The forecast came as a separate report by National Gas said Britain would be more dependent than ever on imported gas for heating.

Dr Deborah Petterson, director of resilience at Neso, said: “A resilient and reliable energy supply is fundamental to our way of life.

“At Neso, we are looking at the upcoming winter and can report this year’s winter outlook sets out the strongest electricity margins in six years.”

However, the quango’s report warns that “there may still be some tight days when we need to use our full range of operational tools”.

It said these could be triggered by “periods of cold weather with low wind, low interconnector imports and high levels of generator outages”.

But Neso also added that grid operators “plan for such occasions and have a range of tools to balance the system should they occur”.

In particular, Neso is increasingly relying on power imports via electricity interconnectors with France, the Netherlands, Belgium, Norway and Denmark to back up the UK’s own power stations.

Interconnectors are high-voltage cables laid across the seabed between Britain and its neighbours. They are typically driven by prices, meaning power is pulled into the UK when prices are higher here than they are in France or elsewhere.

Through a backstop system known as the capacity market, where generators are paid to be on standby when supplies are tight, Neso said it expects “at least” 6.9 gigawatts of imports to be available.

That is up by about 300 megawatts compared to last year, following the commissioning of the Greenlink interconnector with Ireland.

On the other hand, the report warned that Norway – which is connected to Britain via the North Sea Link – was facing below-average water levels in its hydroelectric reservoirs following disappointing rainfall, suggesting it may have less capacity to export power this winter.

Some experts have raised concerns about the way Neso calculates its spare capacity figures, arguing it does not give enough weight to the risk of “dunkelflaute” – periods of cold weather when wind speeds plunge.

Last year, Neso issued an alert to capacity market generators in early January as wind output dropped to less than 10pc of nameplate capacity and grid operators scrambled to avoid blackouts.

Neso has always insisted that at no point were supplies close to failing and that it had been holding around 1.4 gigawatts of emergency reserves that went unused.

But Kathryn Porter, an independent energy analyst, accused the quango of overstating the available wind power and warned that relying on interconnectors to bridge the gap during cold spells was risky.

She said: “If you took the interconnectors off in their entirety, that’s pretty much the whole of Neso’s margin gone.

“If we had a dunkelflaute covering all of northern Europe, then all bets are off.”

Separately, a similar winter outlook from National Gas – which manages the gas transmission system – said gas supplies this winter were expected to be “secure” but that the amount of spare capacity in the system had shrunk by a third.

National Gas blamed declining gas supplies from the North Sea and said the UK was increasingly dependent on pipeline supplies from Norway and shipments of liquefied natural gas (LNG) from abroad.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Aston Martin is dangerously close to running out of road
data.description:
On a day when both the FTSE 100 and FTSE 250 indices opened broadly flat, the share price of Aston Martin stood out like a beat-up old banger in Harrods’ car park – and with good reason.

Observers may recall that this is a company that likened itself to super car royalty Ferrari when it joined the London stock market seven years ago – a comparison that looks more ridiculous with almost every passing day.

While the Italian carmaker flew off the starting grid – its shares quadrupling in value – Aston Martin went immediately into reverse and has stayed there, with 98pc of its value evaporating in a cloud of petrol fumes.

“More Johnny English than James Bond,” broker AJ Bell quipped – but even that feels generous.

Amid an endless stream of missed forecasts and cash calls, being an investor in Aston Martin must feel like one long, inescapable episode of the Wacky Races with Muttley’s raspy snigger ringing in their ears.

Yet the latest profit warning is no laughing matter, especially for the credibility of newish chief executive Adrian Hallmark.

Appointed in March 2024, this is the second profits howler this year, to add to the other two that fell within quick succession during his first year in the driver’s seat.

This one, which wiped another 8pc off the company’s share price, may be the grimmest of the lot.

Sales are heading for a fairly ugly fall by the sounds of it. Despite several new models due to come off the production line, the company is forecasting a “mid-high single-digit percentage” decline in wholesale volumes for the year to December – steep for an outfit that only sold 6,030 cars last year.

Losses are now expected to be greater this year than the £110m it had previously predicted, which was already at the lower end of expectations.

Even more concerning is the revelation that it no longer expects free cash flow to be in positive territory in the second half of its financial year.

It is an admission that raises the prospect of Aston Martin having to tap investors yet again for emergency funds, at which point surely there needs to be an honest conversation about whether the company will ever be financially viable without repeated shareholder handouts.

The Aston Martin show has been kept on the road thanks to the exceptional generosity of those that, for reasons not entirely clear, still own Aston Martin stock – chief among them is executive chair and majority shareholder Lawrence Stroll – plus, of course, the debt markets.

Since Stroll arrived on the scene in January 2020 with a promise to end the constant car crashes, it has tapped investors for £1.9bn via no fewer than seven fund-raisings.

Billions more have been raised from banks and bondholders – often on the sort of punitive terms that only the most financially-stretched businesses find themselves having to pay.

Though several refinancings have helped bring down some of its borrowing costs, indebtedness has shot up leaving the company with net debt of £1.5bn as of June 2025 – a 43pc jump in just six months.

The reasons given for the latest setback are numerous, covering the full gamut of macro-economic headwinds and external shocks from US tariffs disruption and new taxes in China to even pressure on its suppliers from the JLR cyber attack.

The impact of Trump’s trade war on the car industry has been unquestionably severe, despite Sir Keir’s Starmer’s claims that he negotiated a good deal with America, and Aston Martin is particularly exposed because it has no US manufacturing base.

However, its problems pre-date the President’s second term by many years so the excuses are somewhat unconvincing to say the least.

As analysts have pointed out, one of the biggest challenges Aston Martin consistently faces is that demand for its cars rarely lives up to expectations.

That’s potentially a much bigger issue. With losses, cashflow and debt all going in the wrong direction too, there is risk that shareholders and lenders become increasingly reluctant to ride to the rescue.

But the biggest problem for Aston Martin is that it seems unable to make cars profitably. Since going public in 2017, it has racked up almost £2bn of losses, despite selling nearly 48,000 cars and generating turnover of £9.7bn.

The impression certainly isn’t of a company that is “on track” to be fixed by the end of 2026, as Hallmark claimed at the start of the summer.

On the contrary, questions will be asked as to whether management is in danger of losing control given that as recently as April, the company was telling the City it would achieve a landmark profit in 2025.

The pause in deliveries from US tariffs in the second quarter prompted a rethink to forecasts in July, but even then the carmaker’s revised outlook was for it to break-even this year – a far cry from the heavy losses that are now being warned of.

Plans for further cost cuts and delays to spending will help mitigate the hit but only at the edges one suspects, and certainly not enough to quell the suspicion that Aston Martin is dangerously close to running out of road.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: BBC staff take 20,000 sick days for mental health
data.description:
BBC staff took more than 20,000 days off work for mental health reasons last year, new figures reveal.

Employees at the taxpayer-funded broadcaster took 20,884 sick days in the year to the end of March for psychological reasons including stress, depression and anxiety.

That represents just under a fifth of the total sickness absence taken last year, according to figures obtained under the Freedom of Information Act.

However, the real figure is likely to be higher because no reason was given for more than half of the sick days taken. Physical illness accounted for just over 36,000 days.

Sick leave has surged to a record high across Britain. Figures released last month showed UK workers missed 9.4 working days on average last year because of sickness.

By this measure, the BBC is below average, with employees in its public service broadcasting division taking 6.7 sick days each. The figures do not include staff in the corporation’s commercial arm, which is not funded by the licence fee.

But the absences come amid repeated rounds of job and programming cuts at the BBC, which is scrambling to save £700m to plug a black hole in its finances.

The broadcaster said in January that it will cut 130 jobs from the World Service in a move that sparked concerns about Britain’s ability to counter propaganda from Russian and Chinese state media.

That followed hundreds of other redundancies across the BBC’s news output that has affected flagship programmes including Newsnight.

A recent email to BBC journalists, seen by The Telegraph, outlined a further slate of cost-saving measures, including stricter rules for staff around managing leave, taking taxis and attending events.

Bosses said: “It’s no secret that we are under a lot of financial pressure and it is in all our interests to reduce discretionary spend where we can.”

In addition to the cutbacks, the BBC has also been rocked by a string of recent scandals, including criticism of its coverage of the Gaza conflict and its broadcasting of anti-Semitic comments by punk duo Bob Vylan at Glastonbury, as well as misconduct allegations at MasterChef.

Tim Davie, the director general, has survived calls for his resignation after Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, took aim at a “series of catastrophic failures” by the broadcaster.

The BBC chief is now locked in discussions with the Government over the future of the licence fee funding model ahead of the end of the current charter period in 2027.

He has called for an end to “grinding” cuts at the corporation after a series of freezes and cuts to the licence fee saw its income plunge by 30pc in real terms between 2010 and 2020.

A BBC spokesman said: “While this level of absence broadly reflects what we see happening across the UK, the welfare of our staff is of paramount importance, and we have a wide range of measures in place to support people who need it.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Bank stocks jump by £5bn after car finance compensation ruling
data.description:
More than £5bn has been added to the value of Britain’s biggest banks after the City regulator’s car finance compensation pot was smaller than expected.

Close Brothers, the FTSE 250 lender, rose by as much as 10.3pc on Wednesday after the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) estimated that its mis-selling redress scheme could cost lenders £8.2bn in compensation.

Lenders had initially feared they could be forced to pay between £9bn and £18bn to customers who were mis-sold car finance.

Lloyds Banking Group, the UK’s biggest car finance lender, saw its market capitalisation jump by more than £1bn in early trading.

In an announcement on Tuesday, the FCA said compensation payouts for around 14 million unfair motor finance deals could begin next year with individual motorists receiving an average of £700.

Robert Noble, a Deutsche Bank analyst, said the FCA’s plan was “towards the better end of expectations”.

At least four million car finance deals are estimated to have already been subject to a complaint, meaning a further 10 million could still be raised.

The FCA said the £8.2bn total cost to lenders was based on about 85pc of eligible drivers taking part in the scheme.

The figure rises to £9.7bn if all eligible consumers are compensated, which it said was unlikely.

The FCA plans could deal a £850m blow to Lloyds, according to analysts, while Santander, Barclays and Close Brothers are expected to lose £350m, £80m and £170m respectively.

Banking stocks had been hit hard by the car finance mis-selling scandal over the past year.

The redress scheme was confirmed in the wake of a Supreme Court decision in August, which ruled that customers had been treated unfairly. This lawsuit arose from a salesman being paid a 55pc commission for arranging a loan.

However, judges rejected two out of the three cases brought forward by motorists, which contended that all deals involving “secret” commissions were unjust. This limited the scope for payouts.

The FCA decided to act after studying data from 32m car finance agreements struck between 2007 and 2024.

The regulator found that car finance firms broke the rules by not properly informing customers about commissions paid by lenders to the car dealers that sold them the loan.

This meant that many motorists did not have the opportunity to negotiate or find a better deal and may have paid a higher interest rate for their loan as a result.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Miliband refused to discuss North Sea with me, says Ratcliffe
data.description:
Sir Jim Ratcliffe was stonewalled by Ed Miliband when he asked the Energy Secretary to discuss his policies on net zero and the North Sea, the industrialist has said.

The billionaire owner of chemicals giant Ineos said Mr Miliband refused to engage in a discussion about Labour’s North Sea drilling ban in a call with industry bosses.

Sir Jim said: “I was on a video call with him and I asked him whether he could explain his policy on energy and the North Sea, but he wasn’t prepared to engage. He said, ‘I’m sorry, but that’s my policy. I’m not prepared to talk about it’.

“I haven’t tried to meet him again – there’s no point in wasting my time.”

Mr Miliband’s policies are under growing scrutiny after data showed Britain’s businesses continued to pay the highest energy prices in the world for the second consecutive year.

The Energy Secretary has been accused of driving up electricity costs by adding green levies on to bills to fund renewables.

This week Ineos said it had cut a fifth of jobs at its Hull chemical plant, blaming cheap Chinese imports and energy costs linked to so-called carbon taxes.

The company has warned its Grangemouth chemicals plant also faces closure after shutting its oil refinery on the same site last year.

Mr Miliband has repeatedly stressed that net zero was not to blame for soaring prices. However, critics have warned his plans to switch Britain’s power system to 95pc clean energy by 2030 risks driving up prices even further.

Sir Jim said it was time for the Government to start listening to business or risk deindustrialisation in Britain.

He said: “I am calling on politicians to make an 11th hour intervention and save the chemical industry.

“Carbon taxes are costing the Grangemouth plant £130m a year – the plant is stressed.

“The site is the size of the City of London and we need a lot of cash just to maintain it. But that money is going to the Government.

“If these carbon taxes remain they will eventually shut down all the chemical plants in Europe, and the products will be replaced by either American or Chinese imports.”

It came as economists warned Britain will lose most of its chemical industry by 2030 unless Mr Miliband lowers green levies on energy.

The report by Oxford Economics, commissioned by Ineos, found that high energy costs has led to a 30pc decline in UK chemical production since 2021, with investment spending by European firms at half the level of their US counterparts over the past five years.

“European policymakers face a critical decision: act decisively now to safeguard this vital strategic industry or risk its irreversible decline,” the report said.

“If European chemical production is replaced by imports from China and the US, total carbon emissions will rise. Chinese and US chemical industries emit 2-3 times more carbon for the same volume of output than those in Europe.”

Sir Jim added: “We urgently need to bring the continent’s energy and carbon taxes in line with the rest of the world and challenge one-sided tariffs. If this isn’t done, there won’t be a chemical industry left to save.”

The closure of Ineos’s Grangemouth chemical plant would also likely deal a major blow to the UK’s energy security.

The chemical plant is also the terminus for the North Sea Forties Pipeline system which brings ashore the oil and gas from about 80 offshore fields, about 40pc of the UK’s entire output.

Grangemouth’s “cracker” facility strips out the gas, feeding it into the UK transmission network and then pumps the oil to UK users or into tankers, meaning the pipeline would become redundant if the factory were to close.

A government spokesman said: “We know this is a tough time for our chemicals industry, who are paying the fossil fuel penalty, with wholesale gas costs remaining 75pc above their levels before Russia invaded Ukraine.

“Ministers regularly meet representatives of the chemicals industry including Ineos, and will continue to engage with the company and the wider sector to explore potential solutions that will ensure a viable industry in the UK.”
data.author: The telegraph
